speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Martha Lowry Long took place at her home in Kendrick on November 18th, 1976. Her husband Marvin Long also took part in the conversation. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think Roy Land was just saying this morning that your store was really good for people, for credit. Oh, yeah. We had a signature. We traded it. See, we bought it in Seoul. It still belongs to my sister -in -law out here at our feed yard. There's this one right out of town out here. I don't know what that sign's called. They took that down to the fridge. They used it on the float thing, but I don't know where they put it back. That was a wholesome sign. Yeah.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: It didn't belong to us?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: It was a wholesome, very close sign.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From year to year, did you find that you could count on getting all the credit that you gave paid back by the farmers?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, pretty well. We had our spread charge off. We figured we'd keep it under 5%. We were doing all right. Charge under 5%. We didn't charge any more than that though. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you carry these folks for a whole year at a time?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, sure. That's what I thought. Longer than that. I don't think they had anything back on them, but that's where we made our money. We were dealing trading now. Trading. Cattlemen. All the old ridiculous things. Dried brooms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Dried brooms. Dried brooms, yeah. There were anything. Barters. Lots of barters in those days, you know. Had to be doing this. Had a clean bar, up your own hill, drying the brooms. That family, they had a big family. We lived off of that. We couldn't have the crews every year. They'd take care of that cattle. We'd make it, try to get crews and sell all the goods. Sell them on. To other leaders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that the proportion of the business that was trade instead of cash, would it be as much as maybe a quarter of the whole of your sales? Or would that be too much?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I imagine the half maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? In the early days this would be?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, it has more than 20 early days, but no. Boy, that's a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you just pay like a regular market price on what it would get?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We'd have a lot of marketing too. We took in everything, you know. The ranch. That's how we got a ranch. We got a ranch out there. We got the trades. We traded anything. And your brother owned a ranch in St. Louis. There's three of us boys. It's okay. They took everything. Three of the best boys in my dad. They took four of us. They had to. It's the only way they've arrived. We wouldn't make any money. We'd get the barter. Ranch or anything. We'd take anything. Wood. Cordwood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have trouble converting that to the money that you needed to pay the people, the companies for their goods?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, sometimes. We had a line of credit in those days. Like with our big accounts. We generally had them with the dry goods outfit. We'd have the shoe and all that stuff. We'd cash them groceries. We had a line of credit with a guy that was running the bus and works at that time. He owned it. He had control of it. He was off of access on the 8 % end of his money.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He financed us in the summertime. We'd get enough money to pay the others off. Of course, he would sign a note for his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that ...?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no. It was from Frank Thompson. He was a wholesaler in those days. The old Royston -Murdock got it. Oh yeah. I've heard of it. His name was Royston -Murdock. He owned about half of his country at one time. Half of his country if he owned half. Ballmer must have owned the other half. Well, I don't know. He already did own half. Ballmer was earlier. Ballmer was earlier. Ballmer. That is what I heard. This is through the Depression and 20's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do they go back to bartering during the Depression?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, sure. That's what most of them are talking about. That's what we did. We'd take in a whole cartload of cattle and ship it to Spokane to get some money. When we got up against it, that's what we did. We'd give them a credit set for the cattle and ship the cattle and get cash for it. That's what we kept us some business. We had to have so much money with that. Dad would go out and get them a bread. He'd go out and get us some part of the cattle. Chicken. They'd take chickens and ship them up to Pullman to the city market up there. To the city market.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there a tight period in between the pioneering days and the Depression where people had money to pay?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, sure. That's what I thought. Yeah. So it just kind of reverted, went back to the way it had been when the Depression hit. Well, it was whether it went back or not. Some of them gained back. Some of them made a pile of money on the Depression. They'd grab ranches, accumulated ranches, anything. This Frank Thompson, I think, he always took our notes. And he'd take them, he'd take them to the bank. That's all he'd carry. He was a banker from the... That's how the grocery store fellas kept them going. He kept them financing. You know, the Depression, there wasn't any money.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You've heard of it, haven't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm -hm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you get right up against the edge yourself at the store of having to shut down during that time? Did you always have enough credit? I mean, they would limit your credit too, right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, sure. You just couldn't... You've got to get it figured out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But we always had this edge, this leverage, see?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We had this outside deal. Depends on from when we got it in the jam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which outside deal? To go and get cattle and stuff?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, cattle business. Many grocery stores could have a cattle business on the side. But I had a brother and a father, they didn't know anything about the store business. They didn't want to know. They never did know. I had another brother, older brother, who quit until he passed away. He was meeting the groceries and never really tried to get inside of there no more. But, so, in this cattle business you kept butchering and you would sell meat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You kept selling meat through the store, right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Sure, we shipped cattle, the carloads. Sometimes the carload might go out of here every week. There was quite a country through here, you know. We'd take that in any way we could get it. We had to get all the cash, you know, to barter at these farmers on these cattle. It was a trade deal, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to drive around and sell meat to the farms?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I used to when I was just big enough to go around and open gates. And I wasn't big enough to pay the meat. That's the only reason they took me. Get down and open them gates before they get through.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And his brother?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: His brother would drive this meat wagon and we'd have a wood we'd have. We'd have a box in the back that would be double decked and fill it up with ice in the bottom of it. And we'd have the two decks you'd put the meat on. We'd have a house, house, farm room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what the price of meat was?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Six, eight, and ten. Soup, bones, two bits. Gold soup wine.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What's the point of buying that? What is your picture?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You've got to do a picture of him here. Yeah, wagon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say six, eight, and ten, what does that mean? A pound?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Six cents for barley meat, eight cents for roasts, ten cents for steaks. Deep ole steaks, any kind of steaks. Soup, bones, if there wasn't trim, there was two bits.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he tell me the truth?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, he is. We've got a picture of the ole meat wagon right on the way down. Damn, sure. I guess we... I'd go back and maybe re -pitch more. We were in a different folder. I had it in that box. Ten cents a day during the special. Cute little wagon and they had some ice. Just had some ice when they'd start up in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you have ice?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You had their ice for sure. You'd get it and the women would come out with their big pans and get their whole hunk of meat. Take it and cook it because nobody had refrigeration.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I imagine you'd be able to sell some of the thrashing crews too, right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, yeah, that's when we did it. Yeah, when they were thrashing. When we were harvesting that's the only time we did that. We didn't do that until around... I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you find?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I used to, when I first started that deal, I just opened... I just told them not to go along and couldn't drive a team, you know. I was just told not to go along and open gates. I don't know how to grow. He taped a lot of that for somebody.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Doesn't you, was it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We didn't let you have to be in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, who is it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I can't think where it is right now. When you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was I thinking?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, this meat. Did they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your family raise a lot of that meat themselves? A lot of that cattle? Or was it just mostly buying stuff that was already raised?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Buying stuff and trading, that's what they did. And then, of course, after we got downtown here, there were a few parents out there that launched them a sister -in -law. It's... They had a dad and a brother. They wasn't sold cattle all the time. They was in the business. They were shipping all the time. The priest, they'd get a car load up and ship it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they butcher them or ship them live?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, ship them car load up. Yeah, live. They had a slaughter plant out there. They butchered for the shop. Tom was the meat cutter. He was a professional meat cutter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had said to me, I think, that you cooked in Thrasher and Cruz, didn't you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that just when you were living at Pullman?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, it was when I was living at Pullman that I went and did this, yeah. One time. Was that on...

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Did we do that on that tape?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, you just had mentioned to me that you cooked in Thrasher. Yes, the year I graduated from high school.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Are you taping this now?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I know. Just don't pay any attention to that. The year I graduated from high school, I was 16 and my children were 17. And our brother had a wheat farm down near La Crosse and he wanted cooks. So he was going to hire us. She was the chief cook and I was the assistant. And I was going to college that fall and I needed money real bad. And so I was glad to get a good job like that. And it was out a ways from La Crosse and we had... It seems to me we had 30 men that we cooked for. And he drove a combine and then he hired the other men. It was still...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would have still been horses, wouldn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, I'm sure it was horses in the days of horses. And so there was lots of work and they had to have lots of men. And my job was to set the table and peel the vegetables and wash the dishes and help her. Whatever she needed, she was the head of it, head cook. Now there'd be two or three men in the big outfit. Somebody that wouldn't run the combine, that and the other one. Probably two to keep you away from it. Well, they used combine. Well, I said combine. Well, I think it was a combine. It was a combine. They didn't have a thrashing machine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that work then to be really exhausting?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, well, we were chums, you see, and it was great fun. And then we'd run around all night with boyfriends and get up at the crack of dawn. We had to get up at five o 'clock to get breakfast, you know. No, I don't know. It was great. And then I had money enough to go to college that fall. We got it. It didn't take too long. You don't need much sleep at that age in here. We had time in the daytime. We had some time to rest during the day, too. Oh, yes, we'd get our work done. We served three meals and sometimes we sent out a lunch, but not always. Didn't always have to send out a lunch. But we were so young, you know, and one time the Calumet Baking Powder Company came out with an ad. Calumet Baking Powder was fairly new then. And they came out with this ad for making checkerboard cake. And all that sounded like that was really something. We thought, well, I said, Danno, let's try that for the thrashers. And so we read the directions and we made checkerboard cake for 33 men. And what it amounted to, you made a batter of chocolate and a batter of light. And then you put it in the pan in strips and you baked the two layers like that. And then when you put them together, you reversed one. So when you cut the slice, it was checkerboard. It was really quite an elaborate idea. Your card just gets behind mine. It isn't one that you would choose to serve thrashers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, you wouldn't go to all that trouble. You'd make an easy pan, a cake in a flat pan, cut it in squares and serve it to them to save. They'd like it better. They weren't. That's a family cook. Yeah, it is. The old one now, got in here to go. They don't get, they don't wear out. But you see, I was used to cooking. My mother had died of nose. Well, she died that spring, but she'd been ill a long time and so I'd been cooking at home. They get money for that stuff now, but the price has probably doubled on in the last seven, eight years. Your coats are all Pendleton's, Marvin.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Your coats are all Pendleton's. Well, then they are. Three of them. Quite a while too. No, not very long. They paid some money for them too. Well, I know. You ought to know. That's what I mean. You ought to know how much they cost. We had two from the Crescent and one from Clarkson. When your mother was sick, that must have been really difficult for a whole family. Well, it was. And my two older sisters graduated from college and went to teach. And then I was the oldest one yet at home. And the next sister was five years, four years younger than I was. But we kept house for Dad and some of the boys were still home. And my oldest brother came back and worked and helped the family, support the family too. But we did, both of us, all of us when we were going to college, we did all the work at home. Went to college too. Canned in the summer and did what was necessary during the day. Never thought anything about it. Just something you had to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that she died of?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Cancer. I mean, that was in 1920 that she died. They didn't have all these new treatments.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were going to high school and keeping house too?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, we did all the work at home through high school, yes. My oldest sister was, my second sister was still at home when I was in high school. But she graduated in 21, so she was just there one year after I started college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was school like for you then in Pullman? You mean college or high school?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, high school. Oh, well, Pullman had one high school and it was just a few blocks from the house we lived in after I finished high school. I lived on the other hill. Pullman is built on three or four hills. Really four hills. High school was on the one we lived on when I was in college. And then I walked all that distance to college. But when I was in high school, I lived over on the one they call Pioneer Heights now. And then they had two grade schools. And then this, what they call Sunnyside School, it was a big ornate building, kind of a Victorian building there next to the high school. And at one time it had been the high school too, I think. I know it had before they built the new high school. It had been in that building along with the seventh and eighth grades. And that's where I went to seventh and eighth grade, in that building. And then there were two other grade schools. I started school at the Edison School which was on College Hill. My sixth year, that's when we went to Pullman, I was in the sixth grade. Then I had seventh and eighth in this building. And then high school in the Westnoe Gymnasium, we went to the college for all our athletic events. We had them in the college gym or basketball games. Our high school physical education, such as it was, it wasn't regular classes, but we played basketball. And that was in the top story of this old building. The third floor was turned into a, it was just a small, it wasn't regulation. So they couldn't have their regular basketball games there, but the girls had their games there. I played basketball in my girls team, but just within the high school we didn't play other schools. And then all of the plays, operettas, and commencement were held in the college Brian Hall Auditorium. Because that high school didn't have an auditorium, it just had a big study hall. And it was suitable, it had a small stage, it was suitable for small plays and class day events and things like that. I was in Brian Hall last night. But Brian Hall is where I graduated from high school and college both. Because the high school at that time didn't have a gymnasium even like most schools would have had to conduct it in. I'm thinking of the home ec in high school when you were going there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get that stuff in high school?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes. They had a real good laboratory at Pullman, old fashioned. It had, in my time it had progressed from domestic science to home economics already. It was called domestic science in the old age, you know, when it first started. But when I was in high school it was home economics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the thinking of the change of the names?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I don't know exactly. But it started as domestic science, just like the name implies. And then home economics was a little broader in concept. And we had even 8th grade home economics once a week. And I learned to make, of course I cooked at home, but that's when I learned that some of my mother's New England terms were not the thing that you did. And it embarrassed me when I found out that Johnny Cake was cornbread and a spider was a frying pan. I wouldn't have admitted to anybody that that wasn't what we called them at home. So I learned to make cornbread instead of Johnny Cake. My family thought it was darn good and it tasted quite a bit like my mother's Johnny Cake. And now I appreciate those New England terms, you know, which I think are the South made cornbread or corn foam, but New England made Johnny Cake.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And those are lovely, those regional terms are lovely, aren't they?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But I didn't appreciate that as a teenager, you know. That's the age when you become, oh, lots of people become ashamed of their parents' old fashioned ways. There isn't any understanding, but I never admitted to the teachers that I was accustomed to eating Johnny Cake.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there clothing and practical stuff in high school too?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, it was called Cooking and Sewing. The classes that you took were Cooking and Sewing and they had a very good old time style cooking laboratory. Which was a hollow square and it had zinc metal tops on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you call it Roam -like knobs?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I think they did when I was in, seems to me they did when I was in high school. It had zinc metal for the countertops, which was used quite a bit even in home kitchens. And in the corner of this hollow square there was a sink, a small sink in each corner. And then on each side there would be two pairs of students. And they would, so that four girls were using the sink, you see, when they needed to. And then we had hot plates. And then there wasn't a stove for baking. I don't remember whether it was an electric, I think it was an electric stove. It was just about the time the electric stoves were coming in. But we had two burner hot plates. And so the combination of this plumbing in the corner and the electrical units and the zinc top. When the teacher would leave the room, the girls would take hands, both hands clear around that hollow square. We were on the outside of it. And somebody would rub that zinc and you'd get a real old shock. Of course we weren't supposed to do that, but when the teacher was out we used to do that. And that was because of the grounding, some way. The person on the end would rub the zinc and it would go through everything. Yeah, and it would go clear around the circle. Oh, the teacher catches sometimes. We weren't supposed to do that. It was a little dangerous, probably a little dangerous. I don't know, we never got a big enough job to hurt us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like in sewing, were there certain styles that the kids were being taught to do, like modern or was it more of a hole spun?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, in high school we didn't get very far in the actual clothing. We were in stitches, you know. And we made aprons. I know I made an apron. And I bought Czech gingham. My mother knew that there was Czech gingham down at Emerson's store for five cents a yard. And that's about all the money we had to spend for it. And she sent me to get a yard of this Czech gingham. It was the cheapest material there was and it was a real fine check and it hurt the teacher's eyes and she complained about it. And she had no understanding that some child had to buy that just because it was cheap and I did cross stitching on these Czechs. See, that was the project that I had children and it made a real attractive apron. But it hurt her eyes to help me with my sewing. And of course she didn't understand that there was such a thing as somebody that had to buy a nickel apron.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you want kids to use for their apron?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh well, it could be any kind of gingham but a little better quality. The patterns wouldn't have affected her that way. She was a good teacher. Sounds a bit stuffy to me. Well, she just didn't understand the problems of a poor family. California English walnuts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that? Are they grown here?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, they're grown in two years. She didn't have very big crops this year. Some of them are that quite rough. The teacher always wore white aprons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the class?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, she wore a uniform maybe. In the Pullman High School. When I went out to teach, I had to wear a white apron. I always did. You didn't ever consider wearing a colored apron. I teach home economics. And the girls wore aprons. But not big coverall aprons. They just wore them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the teacher wear a coverall apron?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, she wore a uniform I think. She was a full time home economics teacher in that school. When you say uniform, I think your verses are uniform. Well, I mean like a smock. Like a completely...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there very strict dress standards when we went to high school in Pullman? What high school students should be wearing?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I don't think it ever came up. The styles were not... there wasn't anything... ...that... any reason for it. Really. I don't remember anything like... ...below your knees and nobody ever thought of having... we were modest.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And I don't think there were any of the... like wearing pants or anything. The girls didn't do that. For them to hike in and you had... you had gym bloomers and boy were they something. They were... the ones I had in college even. The ones we had in high school we made out of black satin. They were the ones that were... No, they were big and full because they weren't supposed to look like pants of course. And then they had a band just below your knee but because of putting elastic in there would restrict your blood supply and that wasn't healthy. So they had tapes inside these bluers from the band up to your waist. And it was quite a trick to get into those things and have the tapes in the right places. You know, you'd get all twisted up and if you're in a hurry that was a real trick. That's the kind of... that I wore in college for physical education classes. The tapes were to hold them up. Hold them up because otherwise they would have fallen down straight and you wanted them blousy so they looked like a skirt. They were sort of pleated at the waistline and then you wore a white midi with that black. In other words you used your own garter shirt. No, there wasn't a garter. It wasn't a garter. It was just plain cotton tape to hold that up. Yeah, they held it right up to the waist. It held the cuff up so that they bloused a little. Did the tape...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: did it fasten here or did it go around?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, it was just stitched to the band so that it made them look like a pleated skirt at a glance. And then they gave you the freedom to modestly play games and play basketball. We wore those all through my college days in gym class. And there was no... and stockings. There were no shorts or anything like that yet for girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was never appropriate for a girl to wear pants in the last day?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bloomers?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We did wear hiking pants a little later perhaps but I don't think at that time I did. Except that is bloomers. Yeah, bloomers and black sateen and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the black sateen?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Black sateen is a fabric that has... it's a cotton fabric that has a weave, something like satin weave. But it's cotton and it has the most peculiar odor. Typical of satin, I don't know why it has but it does and it never gets over it. The more you wash it the more it always smells like that. You mentioned too that you girls went through college but the boys didn't. Finally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean that the boys were actually helping put you through college?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well... no. It was because girls couldn't... one thing was that my oldest brother had two years of college and then the war came, first world war and he went to the navy. And then my mother got this cancer and when he came back then he had to... he couldn't go on, he had to help the family. And he got a job and started working the post office. And my second brother was just... it was partly because of mother's illness. He lost his interest in school, you know, as teenagers often do. And then my youngest brother, well, he just needed more guidance but they all turned out real good and had good jobs. My youngest brother ended up in the State Highway Department of Olympia. They're all dead now. There's four rest boys, we need to go to school once in a while. A year, maybe half a year. But the girls couldn't get jobs, for one thing, you know. If you could train to teach then you could get a good job. But if you... the boys could get better jobs, that was partly the reason too. So it was more attractive to not go on to school, to quit and earn the career. Well, they could get work. Well, were all the girls in your family...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: they all went to college, right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They all graduated. The WSC was in those days. They all took Home Economics. They all taught. It was part of their thinking that they all wanted to be able to make a good living for themselves to be sure they had it. Well,

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: yes, it was darn money, of course. We worked to go through but we had to do... Oh, we worked... several of us worked in the telephone office. That was one job you could get in Pullman. You could do housework for women and you could babysit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was a four year program, the Home Act, at that time? In college, are you talking about?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, yes. Pullman had... it had started, you know, as an agricultural college and then it had become a state college at the time that we attended. And Home Economics was one of the big departments. They had a good department. Well, so it sounds to me like... I would think now, if you were doing that, that a lot of people would do it feeling it was going to be a career. Oh, well, sure. Sure. Teacher. Teachers. We were all training to be teachers. And that was the field we chose. That's true. And it was just opening up in high schools, too. A lot of high schools were just putting it in. Yes, in those years. My oldest sister taught here in 1918 and then it was just a beginning thing. And I went to cul -de -sac. It was my first school.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I guess that was the first time they were out of the year, wasn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I don't know. I rather think it might have been. And it was a cul -de -sac. They didn't even have any equipment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No. They just knew that they wanted Home Economics. And they didn't know they wanted this for the girls. But they didn't... the school board didn't understand that there had to be any laboratory or anything. And there was a room set aside for this. And in that room... I don't know what they'd been using it for because they hadn't had a Home Economics teacher. They had an old wood -burned cook stove and a cupboard full of odds and ends of dishes that people had donated. And lots of them were chipped. And a treadle sewing machine. And that was it. And I was supposed to teach Home Economics.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And they finally got me a... I don't think they ever got an electric range. I can't remember that they did. They finally got a... there were no measuring cups or measuring spoons. And we were taught how to do it that way. You measured... you didn't do it with a handful of flour and a pinch of salt and all that. That wasn't the way we were taught. That wasn't Home Economics. That's the way your mother and your grandmother cooked. And they were good cooks. But then that wasn't what we were sent out to teach. And they had... they didn't have any of those things. And... they finally that year, they got me some. We had just a big long table. There was no laboratory set up. But they did have a big table that was suitable. The girls could stand around it and prepare the recipes for the particular day. And it was also used for the sewing work. They could cut out their garments on this big table too. They did finally get me an electric sewing machine and that was quite a help. Although the girls... I always made them learn both because at home a lot of them had treadle machines. They needed to know how to use a treadle machine. That was in 1924. And electric sewing machines were just coming into the homes, I guess. But they learned... Was there a stove...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: there was a cook stove in there?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: There was a cook stove. A wood burning stove. That's what most of the girls were used to. So that wasn't too bad. They all had to cook on this one... their little dabs of stuff on this one stove. Because they didn't cook quantities. They cooked small amounts, you know.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But they learned stitches first, you know, how to sew them using a thimble. And they had to wear an apron to cook with and they had to learn, well, accepted customs that weren't always... And that was one of the problems of being a teacher. You had to remember that things that their mother did... You mustn't talk down... that was important. Like tasting out of the spoon you were stirring with. Most women did that in the homes, but then the girls had to learn you didn't do that. That wasn't sanitary and you didn't taste. You had a separate spoon to taste with. I don't know how they came out of the home. I always hoped they didn't criticize their mothers too much. Because I remembered about my Johnny Cake and the things that I didn't approve that my mother had done, you know. And that's a bad age, that teenage group. But they learned to make the basic... There were no packaged products in those days. We taught basic recipes and how to make everything from... as we call it nowadays, from scratch. They learned to can. We always did that in the fall first. And in a town like cul -de -sac. And it must have been the same here when my sister taught here. The problem of getting something to work with was very difficult because the stores didn't carry any fresh produce. And getting something to can with was a problem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was canning by that time being done by the rural women?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yeah. Oh yes gracious. Women had always canned. The methods had changed a little over the years. My mother canned what we called open kettle. Where it was cooked in a kettle and then poured into a hot jar with a knife stuck in the jar so you didn't break the jar and then sealed. And hopefully it kept. And that was fair. The jar was boiled first. It was fair sterilization. But in my teaching day then we had progressed to the water bath canning. And we just did fruits. We never taught them to can. I never taught them to can. These high school girls used a pressure cooker. Although a pressure cooker for vegetables was already the accepted way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the knife stop the jars from breaking?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That was the theory. And I guess it does do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does it do?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I don't know exactly what it does. Two quart jars you would put a silver table knife in there. And something about the metal.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I hadn't heard it before. That was an old accepted. But we taught water bath canning. That is where you filled your jars and put on the lids and boiled them. Brought the water to a boil and boiled them a certain length of time. Well it seemed to me that canning at some point got real popular. I don't really know when that was. Well canning goes way back but it used to be more preserving because they didn't know about sterilization. And they used some of these brownstone jars I have out here were canning jars and they were sealed with wax. But in order to keep them it had to be pretty sweet. It had to be more like a preserve. Or they used lots of dried fruit in those days too way back then. The canning never got real popular until these jars came in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah but some of those jars were introduced in 18...what's that one with it has the date on it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: 1880 something. 89 that we and the others we have out there. It's right in the glass. They're self sealing. Oh well there were lots of kinds of jars of course patented afterwards. And now we use a self sealing lid. But those others kept too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they self sealing at all then?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes there was one called Economy Jars that had a clamp. That's been discarded since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the common one at that time?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That was one of them. That was a wide mouth jar that was very popular. Then there was a jam jar at the time I was teaching that had a self sealing lid and that one didn't last too long. And then the Kerr people came out with this two piece lid which is the one we still use. But the mason jars that had the rubber and the zinc lids were the old time and of course they can still be used. They're still safe. You mentioned the way that their mothers did it. Now I know this sounds kind of naive but it seems... If I was thinking about the way things were passed on in the earlier days, I would have imagined that all these kids would have grown up learning how to cook and how to sew just from seeing their mothers do it at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But was that true?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That was true but it improved over the years because companies would come out with something new and they would have instructions. For instance there was a steam cooker that was very popular at a certain period that you put on top of the stove and there was water in the base and it created steam. So they were two conservable cookers they were called. Then later they were abandoned as not being a safe way to can things but the water bath was safer. But the company would come out with instructions and so then there was a new canning method you see that was introduced by the company that sold this contraption. That's one way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But like cooking, didn't the kids come in knowing how to cook pretty well?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: A lot of them didn't know very much about it. They had helped their mothers but they didn't know basic cooking very much. And we taught them a little bit about the chemistry side of it. You know, very elementary but understanding of baking powders and why jelly would gel and things like that. Maybe their mothers didn't understand either. They just did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the balance?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes, yes, nutrition. We did that too. We studied, there were vitamins already when I started teaching. Not as many as we have now. Not as great an understanding of them but we knew the diseases that resulted from a lack of vitamin C and vitamin B and vitamin A. Those were the three. And a little bit about vitamin D. And we knew there was a vitamin E but there wasn't very much known about it yet. At the time I started teaching.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Is or not?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And now the B has been split up a lot and it's all different. It's quite a lot different than what nutrition is. But we knew they had to learn the proteins and carbohydrates and fats made up the diet plus the vitamins. Yes, that was all part of our study in 1924 when I started teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel then or were you taught at OMAC that the old ways were usually the best or was it taught that there was a whole kind of new, a lot of new ways that were progressive that should be done instead of the old ways?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, there were a lot of false ideas about foods, putting foods together and all in our college training. One of our teachers was real good and my son was real good. And it made it interesting, all these things that... And we grew up that way too. My mother wouldn't let us eat cucumbers unless they were soaked in salt and you couldn't have cherries and milk together. Well, those things were all proven false. They were old theories but old ideas that people believed in. And there were a lot of those things. So we tried to teach too that those things had been accepted but they had been proven false.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Now in a small school like either where my sister taught here or when I taught at CaliSAC, you didn't teach just home economics. That was before any consolidation. It was a small school. There were just four teachers in high school. And so we had to teach all kinds of things and most of them we weren't very well prepared to teach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did you teach?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I hate to tell you this but I had to teach Latin. They had to have Latin in school in those days and all I had was high school Latin. So it was pretty rough. Because I wasn't a good Latin student, you know, just a couple of years in high school you don't get too much and I hadn't had any since then. But that was one of the things that was required in high school in order to go on to college. And a lot of these kids hoped to go on to school and they wanted Latin. It was a requirement I think because I remember because I had a big class. First year Latin wasn't so bad. But trying to teach Cesar was really something for an experienced teacher like I was. But those are the kind of problems. I taught history. I taught one course in history and I hadn't had any college history and very little high school history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what else did I teach?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I had girls basketball and by then the girls were playing other teams in Idaho. We would go on trips. That was great fun. But I wasn't trained in physical education at all. Just what I knew from my high school days. And then of course we had it in college too but that wasn't my major. And then I taught glee club.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And I had belonged to the women's glee club in college and had taken some boys and had always sung in the church choir. So that wasn't too hard to do in a small school like that. What they expected of you wasn't too difficult. I thought something else. I didn't think it was. It sounds to me like taking together having to do all these different activities. Some of which you really wanted prepared for. You must have made it very demanding. Yeah, it's terrible. But you didn't know it at the time that that was terrible. You just expected to do it. All the teachers were doing the same thing. There was no one teaching just the thing they were trained in I don't think. Possibly the English teacher had enough. So she didn't have to teach anything else. But the rest of us did. Did you feel sometimes that you were doing a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: that you were really not doing the job that you should have done?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I was always terrified of this Latin. But I'll tell you something. You never know. You don't know what you're accomplishing when you work with young people that age. And it comes back to you fifty years later. I was to a reunion at cul -de -sac. A fifty year class reunion. One of the boys I've had in Latin had gone on to college and had been teaching at Dayton. He was an English teacher and I said, I knew how he liked teaching. He was a real nice boy. I'd been very fond of him. And he said he liked it. It was just great. And he taught him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what did you teach?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And he said he was an English teacher. But he said, I wouldn't have been able to teach English if I hadn't had that Latin class under you. Fifty years later he told me that. And all these years I'd worry about teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you see what happens?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You don't know. Apparently I gave him something. Something that he needed to go on and understand. I don't know whether he ever took any Latin in college. I can ask him that. But he was a bright boy and apparently he got something. See, even though I wasn't a good teacher. You don't know what influence you have on kids. That's one of the great things about teaching. One of the greatest things about teaching. If you live long enough to see some of these students later. I got my first Christmas card today. And we had attended a golden wedding this summer in the park of one of my students at Kodysack in this first year of teaching. And she said in this card, I'll never forget what you taught me in sewing. And she said, I wish my granddaughter could have a teacher like you. See, makes it all worthwhile. Even though you think you haven't accomplished a thing at the time. But you did. You influenced them. I taught her remodeling and she needed that as her children grew up. She told me. Lots of makeovers we called it. Making over clothing. You know to extend it. All families did that in those days. And they still do it. So when she had a family, she needed that because their income was low. And she had learned that in my class, she says. That was one of the things that I, because I had grown up that way, that's one thing I knew how to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were doing that, teaching those kids, did you have any guiding ideas in mind about how, what you were trying to accomplish? Whether or not you knew if they were learning and that sort of thing? You know, was there a, were there something that, for instance, did you know, could you tell whether the kids were really learning what you wanted them to?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, I think so. I don't know. We didn't evaluate things quite like they do nowadays. But, yes, I think I knew. You could tell in work where they made things like clothing and in the food's work, you could tell the results. If they had learned it or if it turned out bad or if they applied themselves, you could tell by half the card. But these testimonials 50 years later, that's very convincing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That you really did something after all, when maybe you thought maybe I do or was it, did I help them?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You know, you're never sure. Kids are kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they in groups as much as they seem to be now?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I mean, it seems like nowadays they're really all, they're together more than they are individuals, you know, when they're at that age. At least that's how it strikes me. I just wondered. Well, in a little town, they're all together. Little town like that, the kids are real. They do everything together in school and out, you know. They have particular chums. There would be two boys, there would be pals, but then they did everything together. It was a fun group. Oh, I thought it was great fun. I enjoyed it immensely. I was young, I was only 20. So I wasn't much older. I had one girl older than I was in fact.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have one grade at a time? Would your class be one grade or would they be two or three together?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, now like in Latin, the first year Latin was freshman. But in home economics, it seems to me it was more mixed. I don't think it necessarily was. Just freshman, it would take the beginning work. Seems to me it was. I'm not real sure, I can't remember for sure about that. You had more than one class in home economics that you taught. No, I just had one. Oh yeah, it was just a small school. And one year I had some boys that wanted to take it. And they had four boys. And they were real good. They had to wear white aprons too. That's interesting. They were sort of butcher type white aprons. Do you think that the And of course, boys are taking it a lot nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it took courage in those days for boys to take it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I don't exactly remember why they took it. But they wanted to and the class wasn't filled up. It was such a small school. There weren't always enough girls to make a full class. And so he let the boys take it. And they were real good at it. And of course that doesn't hurt boys any to learn to. They didn't do much sewing, but they did the cooking part. I can't remember much about the sewing part that I taught them. I taught them some things about pressing clothes and darning and things that boys would need to know. But actual clothing construction, I don't believe they did much of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you shocked by the way when you first got there and saw that you didn't have any equipment to work with?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, I don't remember that I was. I don't know whether I'd been prepared. I can't remember that I was prepared for that exactly. But I had never seen a department where there wasn't anything. But then I was always adventurous and I was willing to try. I taught there three years and then I went to Omak and that was a much bigger school and they had a home economics teacher, but they didn't have much of a department either. And they had me put in a good department. That was one of the things I did while I was there. We started a real good... equipped. Yeah, it's a well equipped. In this first instance, the school board had to be convinced first. You see that it was important to put up any money for anything. And I had to convince them that a sewing machine for instance, an electric sewing machine was needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to go to the board yourself for that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, but then in a little town you soon got acquainted with them. The depot agent was one of them. We all got acquainted real quick. They knew the teachers. They saw that, but they got to know the teachers through the church and through the outside activities. Well, you said when I was here before that the role of the teacher in the community was fairly important. Well, they expected you to stay there and work. I directed the choir in the church extra. I'd always done church choirs. That was fun too. We had a quartet. The bar, the druggist was the tenor and the banker was the bass and the superintendent's wife was the big soprano and I sang all of it when we had a honey of a quartet and it was great fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you sing?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, church. For church. And then sometimes other people would be in the choir too, but then you had a basic choir like a quartet that we could all sing. It made it real interesting. These men in love, the banker and the bar, the druggist, they always came to practice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there other activities that you were expected to take part in?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, there was PTA and that was a bit of a headache in those days. I didn't even know they had it. Oh yes, there was PTA and the teachers hated it, but it didn't amount to very much as far as anything was concerned. It was more just a social evening. The teachers were bored to death. There was never any issues or discussions of any account. It was mostly the parents. It was a chance to meet the parents if they came, but we certainly didn't like it. But we had to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If there were parties and that sort of thing, were you expected to go to them?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, there wasn't anything that you were expected to go to except the high school parties. They had public dances and they didn't object to you going to those if you wanted to, but that wasn't your own business. If you wanted to join the lodge, there were lodges. There was a Rebecca Lodge and we thought it was a good policy. We joined it. We didn't know anything about it. We were too young to be good lodge members, but a couple of us did join them. And the Ku Klux Klan was quite strong and they sent us invitations. We were scared not to and we were scared to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To what? To go?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: To join. The Ku Klux Klan was real strong at that time around. But it was a two -sided proposition. There were some people against it and some for it. We were afraid we'd lose our jobs if we didn't, but we never joined. But we got these invitations through the mail. That was a real... We didn't know what to do. We didn't know who belongs to it and we didn't know who to talk to about it. It was real tricky, but we ended up not joining this other teacher and I that got invitations. Of course, now I know better how to deal with a thing like that. But then we just wanted our jobs awful bad and we were going to please her out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But nothing ever happened? Nothing was ever said? You just didn't do it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, they never followed up and forced us to join or anything. No. Well, one person that I spoke to that has taught really looked at it more in the way that you're sort of saying you really had to try to do what you had to do to keep your job and to please them. And she made it sound like it was really rather difficult sometimes to do that. To make it satisfying. I had trouble. I liked all these people and I liked all the kids and we had fun. I was too far from home to... I lived in Pullman, but it was too far in those days to go home except just for Christmas vacation, Thanksgiving. So there was no problem with me staying there weekends and it was fun to have things to do and the kids would take us on picnics and things, you know. I had a real good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't feel like your conduct was subject to too much inspection?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, we knew very well it was, but there was no problem because I didn't do the things that they said you couldn't do. I didn't smoke. Not many girls did smoke yet, but there were some and that would have been out. If that smoke bar had made you smoke, that wasn't allowed. But that was no problem with me because I didn't. But they didn't object to us going to the public dances. I boarded at a place where there was quite a gang and we'd all go together. They didn't object to that. And I did work in the church, which made me acceptable. And I liked it because I was used to doing that. Well, they did. There were things they would have censored you for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would the situation there compare, say, to the school at Quincy or at Pullman? In that same time, you mean?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Well, it compares... Or, I mean, to when you were going to school there. Well, at Quincy, I think they didn't have home economics, but the school was about the same size. No, the Quincy school was smaller. The graduating classes at Coalesc were bigger, but then I knew that my sisters and brothers had gone through. Of course, I was in lower grades at that time. But the Pullman school was so much bigger that there was no comparison, really, to activities. Both schools now at Coalesc had a strong athletic program. They had basketball that was big. They didn't play football, but basketball was big and supported by the town. They'd had winning teams in the past, and it was just the important thing, as it still is in many schools. And they had other activities. Plays. Teachers had to take turns coaching plays.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I never coached a play. The faculty put on a play once and we all took part in it. I never coached a play because I had this music part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do I have that just one year or two years?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They had music teachers for a band, an instrument, but it seems to me that I had the Green Club two years. And then we would put on skits between acts of the play, so that was my contribution, and I didn't actually coach a play. But some of the teachers had to do that.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And programs for PTA, you had to help with those. They didn't have the old kind of just get -togethers at school anymore there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean parents and kids and all?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No. No. I don't think so. They had school parties, some school parties. I don't think they ever had school dances. I don't believe they danced, but they had school parties where you played games and things sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, when you mentioned the Klan, do you remember any of them doing anything around there? Burning crosses?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, they burned crosses all through this country. They did that up here too, Martindale. But I don't know of any bad things they did. I didn't know of any bad things they did. But like we hear in the South, in the early days. I hadn't heard of people around here that had mentioned the Klan. Some of them had mentioned that they were more anti -Catholic. Well, that wasn't any problem there.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The town wasn't on the reservation, but the area around is the Indian reservation. But I don't think that was an issue with the Klan either. I don't think Indians were. It's a Catholic situation. That wasn't an issue down there. There weren't any Catholics there. There were Catholics at Lapway, but not at Culdesac. There were no Catholics or no churches at Culdesac.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, from there, when did you first become a home demonstration?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I went to Omak from...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they giving to your members?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think I was getting 135 at Culdesac at the end. I think I got a raise. I think I started out at 125 a month. That was for nine months. And I think I was getting 135 and I went to Omak about 145 as I remember. It wasn't a big raise, but it was and it was a bigger school. And it was all working with girls, which was really what I was best trained to do. I was a girls advisor and then more home economics classes and also taught girls chemistry. And of course I'd had chemistry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you board at Culdesac?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes. Yes, teachers boarded. There were no apartments in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about Omak?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And the same at Omak. I boarded. I don't remember any teachers that had apartments.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you pay? About $40 a month?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I paid 35 at Culdesac, I think. I don't remember at Omak. It was about the same. Maybe 45.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that include breakfast and supper too?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Three meals. We lived close enough to school. And your room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that pleasant boarding with you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, yeah. I lived with nice, good families. I lived with a deus family in Omak and it was very pleasant. There were two other teachers that stayed there too.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And we had washing privileges to go to our laundry and pressing and such and such. We were just like members of the family we wanted to be. But the Omak school was much larger and progressive. Very progressive, I would say. They had a class for the boys that was a combination of shop and agriculture. And then they had home economics for the girls and they had a girls league that I was the sponsor of. And the superintendent was really interested in advancing that work in the school. It wasn't vocational home economics yet at that time. Although we were qualified to teach vocational, we were trained at Pullman so that we could teach vocational home economics. But they didn't get a federal help like they do now or did later. It wasn't strictly vocational but it was coming toward that. And with our training and teachers training and all, we were trained to teach vocational if we got into a school where they had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say girls league, what was that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, it was all the girls in high school and it was to teach, it was an extracurricular group. And they had meetings, they learned to conduct business, they had a business organization, you see, with officers. And they learned to do that. That was part of my job to see that they learned how to conduct meetings. I don't know if schools still have it just that way, I doubt if they do, but they also had a student body organization but usually it was boys that were the president and vice president and learned that part. The girls didn't learn it but they learned that in the girls league and then they had dress codes, you asked about that and they did there. They had worked up what was acceptable dress codes and I don't remember now what some of the problems were but there were things that parents approved of that they wanted in that and the girls worked that... We worked that out with the girls and they accepted it, you know. It wasn't a uniform or anything, it was just accepted ways of dressing. So I taught there three years and then the superintendent had gone into the state board that we had there, he was a real fine man and he'd gone into the state office. And so we had a good...the principal had moved up and he was good too but I decided that was long enough for me there and so through this first superintendent I applied for a job at Highline High School on the coast. Now we went over and interviewed them and they gave me a contract but it included cafeteria work and I had no special training and I was a little afraid of the cafeteria. Highline was quite a big school between Tacoma and Seattle. It's real big now I imagine but at that time it was quite much bigger than I was working in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did cafeteria work and why did you do that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I would have to manage the cafeteria and I had never had any experience in that. We used to have a little school lunch at Cody's Sack where we made a kettle of soup and the youngsters washed the dishes and we served them a bowl of soup or a cup of cocoa at noon with their sandwiches that they brought, you know. But that was easy. But managing a real cafeteria wasn't exactly in my line and so I was a little afraid of it and I was waiting, hesitating over this contract before I signed it. It was a good job, a real good job and there would have been quite a bit of teaching at the Placifah cafeteria. And one day someone knocked on my classroom door and it was the assistant director of extension at Pullman and he called me out at the home. He stood in the home and he offered me a job in Wenatchee.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He couldn't offer me the job. I had to go down and interview the county agent first but he wanted me to do that and then if the county agent liked me they wanted me to take this job. It was a brand new job and that's how I got into extension. Just like that. So I went down, so then I went down on the bus to, you could go down in the morning and come back on Saturday and I went down and interviewed this county agent. They'd never had a home demonstration agent in Chelan County but it was in 1930, the spring of 1930 right in the start of the depression you see. And they'd organized, he'd already organized the homemakers council among the farm women and they were really anxious to get an agent. So I had this appointment with him on Saturday and I went to his office and he explained the shortcomings of the office. We'd have to share this room which wasn't as big as this room I don't think. And he also told me that he smoked cigars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would I object to that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And I said, Evans, no I like the smell of cigars and he was quite relieved because I think that had to be understood because I had to sit at a desk here and he sat at one here and we were practically back to back and he was not about to give up these cigars in the office so that was one of the requirements. But anyway, the interview went like that and so then I took the job and I liked him and there was no, I knew nothing about it. I knew nothing about the work. There was no state leader at that time to help me, a woman state leader. They were in between leaders and there was no one. And this county agent knew more about it than I did and he helped me get started and he'd already organized the women. So I worked through this homemakers council and they were just, Chelan County is a little different from many farm areas in that it was strictly almost all orchards and no diversification at all so that rural people in the area, like on a wheat ranch, they usually had chickens and the cows and pigs and the sheep and it could be somewhat self -sufficient but the orchards couldn't because all they raised was apples or fruits, different fruits. Most of them didn't even raise a garden. But they could have raised a garden and a chicken if they wanted.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They had a lot of work to maintain it and it was an expensive business if you weren't getting a crop. So they were in a real bad fix.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have the available land if they wanted to, to say keep a pig or something like that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Sure, they could have found a place for small diversification but most of them were, oh a lot of them were college people that went over there and started these orchards. Oh a lot of the women were, I had two home economics graduates, these rural women that were on this council, but that had been, when they started it was a profitable farming venture and it was just the depression and no market for their fruit was what was following the whole thing up. And you see that was just before Franklin Roosevelt came in with his big program, it did save them then of course but it was real rough for a year or two. I went there in the spring of 30, in June of 30.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then of course he wasn't, when did he go, 32 wasn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: It was when he came in, it was in March of 30. Yes, so it was at the end of my working period is when those programs really, but we had some before that we had WPA and... Oh I think the WPA came in just when Roosevelt did, because Henry Hoover didn't do any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it 32 instead of 33?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well anyway it was June of 30 that I went there. And there were really, if you want to know all about this work that I did, there was this work trying to do something for these people that had these orchards that were well educated but were stuck with all this expense, you see, of maintaining these orchards. And then the women had no money to spend on, and so we set up nutrition programs and tried to teach them things that they could do to help the, and gardens were important but the county agent had charge of gardens so we tried to get them to raise gardens and they did, they put in gardens and that helped their food budget. And a few of them tried cows, milk cow, you know. And then the Red Cross, there was Red Cross flour and a lot of those people that I've just described were dependent on getting free Red Cross flour. And then we'd have baking programs, of course most of them knew how to cook so that part wasn't important with them. But we had a big canning program, they even got out of the habit of that because they'd made enough money. We had a big canning program and we worked with drying, went back to some of the pioneer of customs. We made soap, taught them to make soap from their waste fats and a lot of remodeling work in clothing, we did a lot on mending and remodeling, making over, working in home furnishing because their homes had gotten run down, some of them had nice homes but then without maintenance they were run down. We worked on rugs, hooked and braided rugs and made from rags, you know. And studied along with the making, the technique of making, some of the women knew how and I learned and I didn't know much about that at the time. We did refinishing of old furniture and cane chairs and the 4 -H girls had all these same projects, you see. We worked through the youth into some of the families and then we worked with the women too. I had women's clubs that I worked with. But you could teach the girls and then it came out in the home, you see. They would take their room as a project and they would try to do it without any expense or very little expense. We made dressing tables out of orange crates for instance, orange crates you could get for nothing at the grocery store. And putting two orange crates that have a shelf in them with a board between made a little desk or a little dressing table and then they would cover that with a flounce or sometimes they painted them and made little desks. That sort of thing that they could do that made attractive rooms. And then 4 -H work was quite highly organized by then and they had contests where they put on demonstrations at the fairs or local fair just in the county. And the girls would work up a demonstration how to make a dressing table out of orange crates and that was a way of teaching demonstrations were used for teaching methods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you directing the 4 -H work?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I was directing. Just the county agent and I did the whole thing. He did the gardens and the livestock and the certified potatoes with the boys and that sort of thing and I did the girls work. The girls in gardening were under him but they had home furnishing projects, room improvement we called it for the girls and they had canning projects. The girl would choose what projects she wanted for that year. And they had cooking projects and they had sewing projects. And in the sewing projects again we tried to teach the girls to make over and to make, they learned simple stitches to start with. They came to dish towel for instance. Well the dish towel was a flour sack or sugar sack. And they learned their hemming on that and then they made simple articles, the little ones, the young ones did you know. They had pot holders and then they advanced to clothing and they had style shows finally. And the advanced girls would make elaborate costumes.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And then they would exhibit these at the fairs. Well one girl, one year, a sugar sack was different from a flour sack. It was a larger piece of fabric. This was a 100 pound sugar sack that people bought in those days. And it was a finer fabric and more open weave in a way but it was a finer fabric and this girl took these sugar sacks and bleached them and starched them a bit and made a costume, a complete costume out of sugar sacks that she exhibited at the fair and it was a winning exhibit. And it was loaned to the state clothing specialist who took it all around the state and showed it as an example of what a girl had done with nothing. Something that didn't cost her anything. It looked like a white linen costume when she had it finished. And she made the slip and the panties and the bra, the whole thing out of the same material. That was just one example but they all did, they all had to make garments and there were certain regulations about how they would be made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were all the people that you worked with quite motivated by the difficult times to cut every corner of your costume?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, we really had three. Now I'm talking about these people that owned these orchards now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girls and the women?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, they were from the rural families that lived mostly but in the county there were sections where they had less, where the farms were not as productive and those families then didn't have as much to work with as the others did. And there were two sections south of Wenatchee, the soil wasn't as good so it didn't produce the same. And so there was a little different problem there but most of those families already were more diversified and that helped too. But then in Wenatchee I worked in the whole county, you see we were paid, extension work was cooperative, it was Washington, the federal department of agriculture and the college and the county. It was a three way contribution to our salaries. We worked for all three and so we had to work with all people in the county if they wanted us to. And in Wenatchee at that time there were really three classes of people. The ones I've been talking about were the ones who owned orchards. And then in the city of Wenatchee, which was a big town at that time, a big town, there were people that were the labouring class that were down and out because there was no employment. And so we tried to work with the county, the county had to put up money for them, the county commissioners had to put up money for them to buy groceries with so they were anxious for us to help them so that that would reduce their load there. And so we set up gardens on vacant lots in Wenatchee, that was the county agent's job, he did that. And then I worked with the women on the nutritional problems, tried to, using this free red cross flower that we gave out through our office and tried to teach them too. Well, the fact they were in those circumstances meant that they were not too ambitious to start with, you see, and a lot of them didn't know how to bake bread, some of them didn't. And so we had a program on baking bread. And some of them could get wheat and they used quite a bit of wheat and would grind it or would cook it and make casseroles and all, you know, wheat, unground wheat. If it's cooked long enough it becomes very palatable and makes good, and sometimes they could get that cheaper than they could things they bought at the store. So we worked with that some and we did canning, some canning, but then they didn't have equipment so at that time we weren't canning vegetables in water baths and it was vegetables that were the problem and we tried some drying. The climate was very favorable to drying because Wenatchee has a long hot summer and we did drying of fruits and also of some vegetables. Green beans can be dried but I never thought it was worthwhile but we had directions for it and they never taste very good after they're dried. But corn of course is excellent. We did some of the fruits and some of them made dryers that they used on their stoves or dried in the oven and most of them did sun drying. They did some of that. And then we did some work on clothing, remodeling that would help them in making over things to be a little, circumstances a little better. I didn't do very much home furnishing work with these people. Their homes were very bleak but they didn't have, well, a lot of them probably came there to work in the fruit and then you see when there was no market there wasn't that work in the warehouses and in the orchards that there had been so they were out of work.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: How did you organize that? Did you give classes?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I would have a program with them and once a month I would have a program with them. But this other group in town didn't have that kind of social organization and so we would have a list of people that the county was providing groceries to and we would work through that and get them together in neighborhood groups and have these, on a small scale have these meetings. I wouldn't say it was too successful but then as I told you about these high school students you don't know what was accomplished. You don't really know and hopefully we've helped some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these people, many of them, you think really the migrant labor force?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
