words: Well, some of them probably were. Then there was a third group that I haven't told you about yet that was worse yet. And there was a place down along the Columbia River just at the edge of town across the tracks that was called the jungles. And most towns had jungles in those days where they were mostly the tramps that rode the boxcars there but in Wenatchee a lot of these migrant workers had been caught without anything and they were living down there in any kind of a little shack. They couldn't get away. They didn't have any money to leave with so they were caught there for the winter and they hadn't earned enough to support themselves and they were just existing down there as best they could. There were also tramps and it wasn't a very pleasant area to try to do anything in. A lot of the shacks were built out of cardboard and corrugated metal for a roof and they were just makeshift accommodations. The furnishings were very meager but the county commissioners wanted me to go down there and see what I could do because they couldn't let these people starve. But the chief of police in Wenatchee wouldn't let me go alone even in that day. I wouldn't have been afraid. I didn't know enough to be afraid. But he said no. He said I'll go with you and he always went with me. So that was something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do down there?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well I tried to teach them. For instance they could have this free flour but they had such meager equipment. We tried to teach them to bake bread. And some of them had stoves. You know old stoves were thrown out in those days and they could pick up an old stove and some of them had ovens. We did some work on bread baking with this free flour and taught them to make their own yeast. You know you can get yeast started and then you can keep the starter going. And so you save the expensive yeast and that was one thing I taught them but then I don't know how many of them. You couldn't get results on that work very much. So I don't know how many of them ever used it but that was something they did. We taught them. They didn't have equipment to do much canning but we taught them what we could and drying. They could dry things. And there were plenty of apples available because apples were being dumped. You know there was no market. So that they could have all the apples. In fact the orchardists would bring apples to needy people like that. Because they just dumped them out in the pile and that didn't help the contamination of the orchards any. Apples dumped you know. Because the coddling would flourish. But we worked with this group down there in the jungle. Many of them. The very discouraging. Families yes families.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of condition were the kids in?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Not very good nutritionally of course because they weren't getting a well balanced diet. The county was giving them a little order. I don't remember what it was they gave them. So they existed but it wasn't a very well balanced diet. But if they could help themselves a little. Do some cooking you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do those people seem defeated? Just defeated or desperate to you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well they were desperate. Living down there was that was. And of course. I don't know what happened to them. I suppose times got there. Well when those programs came in they got work. WPA and those work programs you know. And that saved them. But there was a period in there when it was pretty bleak.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they there during the whole time that you were there? Did they stay up until 31?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I didn't work with them. I don't think I worked with them but one year.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That first year I didn't work with them. I didn't get started that well. But it must have been in 31 and 32. I left there and went into state office in 34 and 35. 34 and 35. Just after the Roosevelt had all these programs going in. Because our county agent was real involved with AAA and all this stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those people often arrested in Wenatchee at that time? You mean these that lived down in the jungles?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I don't know. I don't remember about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean stealing and all?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No I don't remember that. I don't think so. They just chased them out from one place to another. Well these people were allowed to stay there. There was no place for them to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They did some places. And later they did clean out all those jungles. And I imagine they cleaned that one out too. When there was work available. But at that time there was just nothing anywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these really mostly just cardboard? Did you really build houses out of cardboard?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes. It's say Kellogg Cornflakes or whatever the big carton was that they used to create the... They had to have a wood frame of some kind to nail them to. But they would... Some of them were just hobbles really. Some of them were shacks. Wooden shacks. I expect that had been there a long time, that jungle. Because the itinerant... an industry like that attracted itinerant people you see. It would have more permanence than say the jungle they had here in Canada for that. Well here there were no buildings. Here it was just a place where they had a pot over the fire over by the railroad tracks between... when they stayed between trains or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were sharing their food with each other?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well I expect some of them did. Probably some of them fought. I don't know. It would be normal. But I didn't know anything about that part of it. I didn't know about that. I just know that I was assigned this job to go down there. It was a hopeless assignment to begin with. But we tried. These working people that... the middle group you talked about who were not doing well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their attitude about that? What's going on? What you could tell?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You went to them... Oh you mean the ones that lived in homes up in town. They were discouraged. They got food orders. Yes they got small food orders but they were real discouraged because most of them wanted to work and couldn't get work. You know. And that's the difference between the ones that want to work and the ones that... Food orders. And they had homes. I don't know how they hung on to those little houses. They didn't live in the good houses of the town. And then there were a lot of people that had good jobs. We had good jobs. We were getting our salary all the time. And there were the business people of Wenatchee were making it alright. They were. They had good stores and all. But of course the economy did depend on the arts. The transient traders were the worst from the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The transient what?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The transient. Yeah. So many came into... they were... well they were migrant workers. They came into work in the fruit. They were trained. Trained to sort. You know that it was the whole valley. I worked in the whole county. Chelan and Enneap and Leavenworth and Feshath and all those towns had big warehouses. See it was all arch of country. And then I worked with a few groups out on the fringe. Up toward Lake Wenatchee there was a group of women that I worked with in the winter that... whose husbands worked in the woods or worked on the highway and they weren't archidists. And they didn't have time in the summer for meetings and I would go up there in the winter. The snow was deep in that country above Leavenworth up around Lake Wenatchee. And I had a group up there. I'd go up there in February usually. And I... now I'm a pansy about driving but I had a car we never put on chains. Unless we got stuck. We never had winter tires. And this snow... the snow banks were way up higher than the car along both sides of the road. They'd just plow up the road. And I would drive up there and in parking it was a bit hazardous but then the men were always home. Because they didn't work at that time. Whatever their work was. It was some kind of logging. And so then when I couldn't get out and I couldn't back my car out they'd come and push me out. I can remember that happening many times so I didn't even have to put on chains to get out of that snow bank. And then I could drive back to Leavenworth without any chains. Oh and I'd be scared to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there people who were down and out like these shack people in other places in the county besides Wenatchee?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I never worked with any. I don't remember if there were. I think they kind of concentrated down there. Wherever they were, trenching workers. The county did furnish... Wherever they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they very receptive to what you were trying to do?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I don't know. I really don't know. I never could tell. They were friendly and they knew I worked for the county and they knew the county was feeding them and so they did cooperate. And I don't know. You know it's hard to know why they did or if they really used much of it. But the county commissioners wanted us to do this and these people were getting a county order. They knew they better. It was partly that kind of an attitude I think. But then most of them were interested in trying to better themselves if they could. I never got too well acquainted with them because I didn't work with them the same as I did the other rural people. My work was mainly with the rural people. Strictly rural people that lived on orchards, tracks, farms. These people, you say there was a council that was set up before you got there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was this council?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They invited somebody from each community. They invited women, the leading... He knew them. He'd been there quite a while, the county agent had. He invited these leading women. And then he picked them out and invited them to a meeting and then they set up their council and elected officers. That was all done before I got there. And the woman who was the president, she was one of these...she was a home economics graduate from some Mideast college. I've forgotten where. But their home, they'd had a very attractive house but it was in badly need of money to repair and refurnish. They were raising a family and so forth so they were in bad circumstances. But she was a good leader. And I went back, they had a 30 year celebration to invite me back. And this same woman was planning a trip to Europe. You see, they snapped...those that could hang on to their orchards snapped out of it and became well off. And she was still working in it 30 years later.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The prosperity had come to the fruit business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time, were most of them struggling to hang on to their orchards?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well yes, because at the expense, some of them pulled out their trees because they couldn't leave them and let the disease take over. The fruit disease, cotton and moth and all, they couldn't do that. And if they raised apples they had to be picked and disposed of. And so there was a lot of expense and they had to be pruned and they had to be fertilized and they had to be sprayed. And the spraying was a big expensive program. And some of them did pull out their orchards. But on the main they came through. There was government loans that came in pretty fast that helped them and all these different programs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their attitude towards your work? Were they very positive, these local rural women?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes, they were real interested in it and they made good contributions too. We used them in the program. A woman that knew how to do something, we would let her teach the others. That's the way extension work functions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking now about the council women or just the women in every place she went?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, in the clubs, well, it was the council women. But I went out and met with these clubs and then there would be other women that would know how to do something real well. It would assist with the teaching of that thing. Something that I didn't. There were a lot of things that I wasn't skillful at that I needed their help. We had a big rug show and some of them were, I knew nothing about rugs. But I learned, I got in and studied and did learn some. But some of them knew how to make hook drugs and pressure drugs and I used them as teachers. And we had a big show and that was something nice they could make for their home that didn't cost them anything because they could use a burlap sack and rags and create. Something new for their home that gave them a little boost, you know. And we had a big show and one time we had a show of, and then we studied other things that they had along with our home furnishing program. Some of them would have things tucked away, old quilts or old hand woven coverlets or something. One year we had a display at one of our meetings, we had a display of hand woven coverlets. We tried to make the program interesting to them and anybody that had one could bring it and display it. And some of them were very old and very beautiful, you know. And then we had a little apron contest along with it, that was the practical part of it. And we had a kit of patterns we passed around and that was something new they could make and apron didn't cost much to make. And then we had a contest and we invited Prudence Penny who was the home economics woman for the Seattle Post -Intelligencer.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And Prudence Penny was her name on the paper, you know. She's probably still there, I don't know, but it was different women at different times like Betty Crocker is and such. But Prudence Penny anyway was a real fine woman at that time and she came over and judged the apron contest and gave a talk. That was real thrilling to these women, you know, to have someone, they all took the, a lot of them took the PI and it was real. To think she would come to our little council meeting and do this for us, she judged the apron and talked about old coverlets. We had a great day, that was one of the best days I think, as far as I was concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the council supposed to be the kind of, do the coordinating of all the women's activities during that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well that was, theoretically, theoretically we tried to do it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so you worked with the council primarily?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, I worked, I went out and gave a demonstration in the clubs at that time. Now it's organized differently here and in most places they work through local leaders but we hadn't come, we hadn't advanced to that stage yet. We tried to and we did some. It was local leaders when I got a woman that knew how to make something good and would teach the others, that was working through a local leader. But I was usually there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the council itself was made up of local leaders, right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The council was made up of... They were the women from these different clubs. Either a representative or a president or whoever. They would select them. And then we had an organization, we had a president and a secretary and we had meetings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the council did? What sorts of things did the council pass or do?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I would prepare a tentative program for the year and there would maybe be choices on it and they would help to decide what we were going to study. That was the main function of it. And then in organizing new groups too, they could help with that. We tried to work in all the communities because it was a county. I was supposed to work in all of them. We tried to get groups in all the counties. And sometimes it would just be, I wouldn't actually be working with a club on a month by month basis, but I would go out and speak at a Grange meeting. Or I would take a 4 -H group. We had to have sponsors for 4 -H work. I would take a team, a demonstration team or a group of girls that could do something to a Kiwanis Club meeting. Kiwanis Club is really supportive of our 4 -H program. We had to have this kind of support for camps and for fairs and everything. And the Chamber of Commerce too, and the Rotary Club, they all helped us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the 4 -H club under the council too at that time?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Not under the council. The 4 -H club was under the extension. And they had their own council of leaders. Each club had an adult leader and those leaders had a council. Well on the average, you say the education level of a number of these women was college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that on the average? Was it a higher education level?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no, not average, but there were some. Which you might not think about. You might not think they would be interested in that kind of a program. But you see, they were and they were the leaders and they still stayed with it. They're still the leaders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of them, or most of them, lifetime residents of the area? Or people who had started the original orchards in the area?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think so. I think most of them lived there a long time. And most of them had been doing pretty well in the 20s until the Depression. Well, these orchards were established so they'd been there. I don't know how long they'd been there. I don't even know how long this kind of agent had been there. I can't remember now. But the orchards were barren, so that takes quite a while. They had been productive and the warehouses were well established and the industry was well established. I don't know when it started, really. I ought to know that, but I don't. Well I think it was just after 1900, if it's like Yakima, from what I know about. It's similar. And then we worked with the Horticultural Society. It was a horticultural society, too. We worked with them and they always put on a show every year. And there was horticulturists that worked out of the Pullman office that came in and helped. And they would have exhibits of apples from different parts of the country shipped in to show that from some areas they'd pack them in baskets. And the varieties would be different from Michigan and Virginia and the different states. And so they always had... And then our part, they always wanted something in the way of using the apples in food. And we would have a display of dishes made from apples and maybe a recipe book of recipes using apples. One year we did that and gave those out at the horticulture show and that was... just been a little extra activity, but it was good. Did you get much guidance from WSC or... Well we had... We didn't have a state leader, as I said, until they... Let's see. I guess we did the last year. I guess they got a state leader the last year I was there. And she was kind of new at the office, too, so we were pretty well set up by then and I didn't use her too much. But we did have what they call specialists. We had one in nutrition and one in clothing and one in home furnishing. And they came in and they helped... They gave... Sometimes they would give the demonstrations themselves to the women that were special. And sometimes they would just help me. But, oh yes, I had help. The state office was set up that way with... And then when we came to meat work, the men, the livestock specialists, we had butchering. We had butchering be for the five North counties. They thought that we ought to know more about cuts of meat. And so we had a butchering be at OMAAC and we all went up there and the nutritionists came and the livestock men from Pullman came. And each one of us, men and women, was supposed to cut up a half of a hog. And we did. You laughed once. Well, the way it was a little... Women didn't know anything about it, you see. They didn't know. They might know cuts of meat as you went into market to buy them. We'd been trained to recognize cuts of meat. But when it came to cutting up a half a hog to get those, that was another story. They helped us a little with some of the sewing, but we had to do most of it. We made head cheese and we had a butchering be. You cut up that hog. That was the end of my work in the Nancy. I went back to the office late that night when the nutrition specialist was writing with me. We drove back. I'd driven up there. And I had a letter on my desk offering me a job at Pullman, so that was the end. I decided to take it. So my knowledge of butchering the pig was wasted, you might say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that they offered you to do in the Pullman?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, well, it was called clothing and home furnishing specialist. I went into the state office. But I don't think we need to go into that job. That was another job. That and I traveled all over the state and did the work with the county people, see, like they'd been doing with me before. But that was in... That was just after I learned to cut up a hog. I had the letter on my desk offering me this job.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
column4: Have you cut up one since?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, I've never cut up one since and I don't think I could. It sounds to me like you had a real lot of responsibility on your shoulders in that job. I think I told you the last time you were here that it was an impossible job and you did the impossible.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Is that the way it sounds to you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That's the way it sounds. Yeah, that's why it was great. It was great. We all loved it. And we had a... Throughout the state, we knew the other people doing the same thing in the other counties. And once a year we got there... Twice a year we got together. We had a 4 -H club camp at Pullman in June. We all went to that with our youngsters and saw each other. And then in January we had just the extension workers had a conference and we had social life there and got to know each other real well. And so there was an exchange between us that helped a great deal. Exchange of ideas. Well, when you teach you have a group of teachers that are like that but in this work you were alone. And that made it... And you worked with all these but you never got real close to these farm... very many of these farm people. You didn't see them often enough. It wasn't the same. You worked with them, you became very fond of some of them that you did get well acquainted with. But it was different. You were sort of loners. You were loners.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But the kids were great. They had great 4 -H club. I helped to chaperone the group to Chicago to the 4 -H congress one year from the state. The Milwaukee Road financed that trip so we didn't have anyone. Milwaukee didn't go through our county so we didn't have anyone on the trip. But we had a state winner that was a Montgomery Ward winner and one of my girls won that. So I got to be a chaperone for that trip. And then later when I was in the state office I went to Washington D .C. to the 4 -H club camp too. And that was great.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work more than a 48 hour week on that job?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh heavens. There was no end. I don't know. We never worked by hours.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We had evening meetings and you might be out at a meeting until 5 or 6 o 'clock sometimes. You didn't get through and then you had to drive back. The office, I don't know. You never counted hours. There was no way to limit it that way. I'd get in. I drove a county car but I would get in and the garage would be closed and I'd have to park my car out where I lived. I lived right near the courthouse but there was no counting of hours. You just worked. And then you judged fares.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Why?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You judged until midnight oftentimes setting up a fare or judging it. And you didn't. You got a holiday once in a while. You got two weeks vacation in the county I think. And regular Christmas days. You didn't get a week like you did when you talked. You got Christmas day and you got Thanksgiving day and 4th of July. You worked year round. I don't know. No one ever thought about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your leisure devoted to then? Did you have, when you had leisure or in the evenings when you weren't working, what did you do?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I belonged to other groups. I belonged to a business and professional women's group in Wenatchee. That was an interesting group of women to know and I belonged to Soroptimist Club which was a service organization. Their meetings were real interesting and I got acquainted with women that were not the women I worked with that were near my age group. And we had a hiking group that used to go on Sundays way up into the mountains of Belk Leavenworth and hike. That was through the business and professional women's club that I got into that and that was good for me. And I made friends there because you see I didn't have any way to make friends with women in Wenatchee unless I belonged to those things. And then I did belong to the Grains but those were the same women I worked with. Those were the same farm families. So that I knew them a little better through Grains than I did through, that was in the evenings. That was just the edge of town the one I belonged to. But your social life was somewhat limited because you worked so many evenings and odd hours that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you decide pretty much yourself what were the most important, the most pressing things to focus on?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I suppose so. We would ask them for things, you know, they would suggest things the women did. They would ask for things they wanted and if it seemed a good choice we would go along with it. They were interested in weaving, they got interested in weaving, hand weaving and of course that's a pretty expensive proposition to get looms but I didn't know anything about weaving. And there was a woman at Paul's boat out of Seattle, a Swedish woman that you could go and learn a little about it from and I went over and spent a weekend with her. She had a small table loom and some of my women bought that and wove things out of rags so there wasn't much expense to it and sold them and had a little profitable business. Now that was a very small thing we did but we tried to, if they were interested in things because we had had this coverlet display and they had learned about, we had studied a little bit about hand woven things and they got interested, you see. So then these few women wanted to do this and this one woman, at least one, made quite a profitable business out of it, making runners and pillows and things that she could sell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did these women in general, well I guess, were there times when you found that you were being challenged, your know how, your educational background and all that? You know, where they were saying we know how to do this better than you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh no, there was never any question about that. They knew that I didn't know everything. There was no, no, no, there was nothing, no, no. They were glad to accept anything I did know and they knew that I knew things they didn't know and then I let them contribute when they did know things. No, there wasn't that. No, I got along real well with them. I didn't pretend to know everything. Because I didn't know everything. I just wondered because you know, the fam, I would think that you could find a conflict between them. Here's someone who's a government official who's trying to tell us what to do. Yes, that's right. Some people might, but I probably wouldn't have gotten the job if I'd been that kind. I didn't know anything. I was very shy. I don't know how you could have been too shy if you had to do all that public work. Well, I get awful scared. I got awful scared in public speaking, but I never let on. And you know, that had been the thing all my life. I was always big and healthy and looked capable, see. And inside I was scared to death. And that's good. That's good. You know that's good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why is it good to be scared to death inside?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Because you do better. You do better. But don't let them know you're scared, see. And you always come out bitter. It's the people that are overconfident that fall flat on their faces. You know that. No, no, it's interesting because I feel scared and I wish I did. Oh, don't ever be ashamed of that. That's good. Don't be overconfident. They're the ones that fall flat on their faces. You're interested, see. It shows you're interested in that. You don't fool other people you're working with. Those women, I didn't fool them one bit. They knew I was scared and they liked me better for it, see.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, now that's the way it looked to me in a way. Because I couldn't help it. I was always scared. I'm still scared. You get scared a little. You have no feelings if you're an astronaut. No. I used to be especially nervous if I had to speak before men because I wasn't accustomed to that. But I would never let on. Men, I think men like businessmen. They like big women, see. I had the advantage. Men always like big women. For some reason, I don't know why. You'd think they would like the little pretty fluffy ones, but that wasn't true. The kind of work I was doing and all. They approved of the fact that I did look big and capable, see. So I was able to work. I really had competition on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Don't put that on tape now. They were so bad off then that they were awful thankful to get it. And they figured it was tax money, see. They figured they were paying taxes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean rationing?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, that wasn't rationing. We didn't have rationing then yet. I was thinking that they got the flower. That was just Red Cross flower. The garden seeds were furnished. The furnished garden seeds, I believe. But this was before rationing. We were with stamps and all that stuff. That was during the war, see. This was before that. This is after World War I and before World War II. This is in between. The Depression was in between the two wars. I don't remember anything else that... Well, apples were available through the archer disks because they just were throwing them out anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that people were really pulling together at that time?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I know they talk about it in hard times around here. Oh, yes. Oh, I should say there was lots of barter going on. There was nothing. You had to pull together and you wouldn't then you're... There was bartering going on of different kinds. There was a moment when I think they were closer than people are. Just like Marvin told you about at the store, the bartering that he was doing. There was bartering going on there between people. I can't think of a good example right off. But right across the river was... The orchards were along the river. This was the Columbia River and that was Douglas County right across the river. And then the wheat country was pretty close there and they got wheat through barter. Some of them had it ground or grounded or used it, as I said, in... We cooked it a long time in Yusef and Castrol. The compression area is when the bar did use that. Interesting, the compression. But like they didn't look at you at all as a government employee. There weren't people that didn't want to associate with, you know, have help from you because you were working for the government. Oh, no. They begged to have one sent in, have a home demonstration sent in. They wanted it. Wanted to have it. And of course I wasn't in their circumstances and they knew that but I don't think there was ever any resentment. They didn't expect me to be. I couldn't have worked there if I was. I wouldn't have been working there if I was, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the salary by the way?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, yeah. I can't remember but it was better than I was getting teaching. Better than teaching, even though it was depression. Oh, yes. Better than I was getting at Omak. I can't remember now what I started out at. But it was better than the county furnished a car.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I had to dress. I dressed better than they did. I had to. It wouldn't have been any point to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't get car expense from them?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They had a county garage. No, I didn't get county expense in the county. They had a county garage and they took care of my car. Serviced it. You must have felt a real sense of accomplishment though too in that kind of work. I would think it would be a direct, you know, if you taught somebody something. Oh, I loved it. It'll always be my home. Annette will always be my home. Oh, I love Kendrick now of course. I just loved Annette.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I was making more money than I had made. I had never had any clothes through my college days. I was able to buy better clothes. I had this one green dress. It was an expensive dress because of my proportions. I couldn't buy cheap clothes. And I wore it out to these meetings with these women. I wore it a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We were working on rugs and one day this woman said, Miss Lowry, aren't you ever going to quit wearing that dress?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: She said, I want it for my rug. It was a beautiful shade of green. It was a wool, fine wool. Just exactly what she needed for her rug and she was waiting patiently for me to give up that dress. And I said, well, I've had to mend it. I'll admit that. I've already had to mend it a bit but I said, I don't know. She never got it. Our 4 -H girl finally got it. It was small and she could make a good dress out of it and I gave it to a girl that needed some help. She made her a real pretty dress out of it because it was beautiful material. You see, she didn't resent the fact that I could have a good dress at all. She was just hoping I'd give it to her for her rug. Oh dear. That was funny. You're talking about remaking. That is something else I would like to ask you about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a standard way or certain things that you generally did when you were remaking a dress into a smaller dress or a pair of pants into a smaller pair of pants?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I know. I've heard the homesteaders talk about doing that all the time. Well, first you would rip up the old garment and be sure the material was worth making over. And then when it was ripped up, you would see what you could make out of it with your pattern. You see, there had to be enough and sometimes you could combine two or you could use the old one for a top and go with a skirt of a different kind and such as that. But there wasn't any. It was just the same as making a new garment. In most respects, you had to use a pattern and you could combine materials. And sometimes you could, my mother used to on the homestead and we had to do these same things again. She would rip braid off an old garment and wash it and straighten it out and sew it back on again on a new garment. And then we did a lot of dyeing to improve or to make two fabrics usable together sometimes. You could dye them in the same dye pot and they'd come out near enough the same color. In the same order, you could work them into a garment, different parts of it. Maybe the sleeves would be a different fabric but they'd be the same color. In that order, you know. We worked with patterns and we had patterns, we had kits of patterns that we passed around so they didn't have to buy patterns. They could cut off the patterns from the kit that I had. We had a kit of infant garments that the USDA had worked out that were quite revolutionary at that time as far as the old style baby clothes were concerned. And these were comfortable, easy to launder and easy to make and supposed to be helpful for the child. We had a kit of those patterns that made up garments that circulated from one club to another and they could cut off the patterns and make garments.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would just stencil the pattern?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, they would cut it off on wrapping paper to get them a set of patterns. They'd make their own set of patterns. And then they could make garments for their children or their grandchildren or whoever needed the little rompers and little garments. We taught them a child can fasten big buttons easier than small buttons and in that day we didn't have all these grippers and things they put on children's clothes now. So they learned things like that using bigger buttons that the small fingers could handle. They couldn't button small buttons. So that it made the child learn faster. It was child development work in a way. But we had patterns for aprons and we had patterns for collars or different units of clothing like that. We had patterns for men's pajamas and shirts. When you had to remake the material, you could make something out of it. So maybe a long -sleeved shirt that you wore out in the elbows, you made a short -sleeved shirt out of it. For example, they turned the collars on men's shirts in those days. They would wear out right around the neck and then the kind of shirts they wore. Then you could turn that collar over. And so the side that showed still looked good. That was one of the tricks. We taught them to darn knit like a knit sweater so that it looked like the original knitting. That was quite a trick but it could be done. They had some matching yarn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the point to try to do it so that she couldn't tell that it was a remake?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, of course. Nobody wanted to remake it so they could disguise it. It's not always possible but if it was still usable, then they were glad to have something to wear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the women, the farm women doing this as much as, I mean, were they very actively involved in remaking the clothes?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They're the ones I'm talking about. That group in town had time but they didn't have the ambition. We did some work with them too, this group that I mentioned that were out of work and were so down and out. They were discouraged. They were really laboring people who were not. They weren't farmers themselves. No, no, no. I don't know what kind of work they had done. They were just discouraged people. There were lots of them there in Wenatchee and the town like that because there was lots of that kind of labor, you see, when the warehouses were all working.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They were not these people that had homes, weren't they?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, there were lots of transient workers too. I don't know what kind of work they had done. They had worked in the warehouses. There were all kinds of work in the orchards and warehouses, you know, when they were flourishing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you feel? Did you feel because of the difficulties that you had growing up, I mean, that you grew up a great deal in Quincy and had to make goo there, that that was, did that come in real handy?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, of course, of course. If I had never lived that way, I wouldn't have understood the problems the same or I couldn't have handled them the same because I knew, I knew all about it. That's the way I had always lived. I could live better without money than I could with, see, because I never had any. I think you weren't used to it.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That makes a difference. If you've always had it, you don't know how to get along without it. So then I could help these people better. We did things, we put emphasis on beauty too, not just utilitarian things, but we studied the art side of home furnishing and flower arranging and harmonizing colors in the rooms. The girls, there was big emphasis on their... Well, it's a lot easier to teach it than the practices sometimes, you know. I was going to practice today.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Did you see this picture back there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I hadn't even noticed it. I just came in and sat down. It's a nice picture.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Where did you tell her that was?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I know I've seen that house someplace before. You're sitting in our house. One of the garden club women painted that for Flower Show this year. Our house had been chosen for that bicentennial project. This was part of the staging of the Flower Show. Then she gave it the watercolor and then she gave it to us. It's beautiful. We finally got it framed this fall. It's lovely. It would be nice to have a niece's nephew slide over someday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really difficult for you to spend money when you got it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, I didn't mean that. I thought you said it was easier for you to live without it. Well, I knew how to live without it is what I was saying. So I could help these people because I knew that they didn't have any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was their situation really similar to the one you had experienced in Quincy?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, not exactly. I don't think it was very much like it. It wasn't like pioneering. The need for these same practices like making soap, anything you could do that didn't cost you anything. If you could provide your laundry soap, they made good laundry soap. We taught them to make toilet soaps, scented and clothing soaps too. One of the women at Pullman, the woman in charge of our teacher training, had worked out some recipes and had experimented a good deal herself and had worked this all out. When we would go to Concord, she'd have a class for us and teach us this. So we could teach them to make toilet soaps and some of the women did make very satisfactory ones, but they could all make good laundry soap. And there was quite a saving because they had some waste pads. Whether it made or it was not. It wasn't the same if they didn't butcher as it was if they butchered, but they got so that they began keeping animals and butchering. And then you had plenty of waste pads. That's when you could really make soap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any raised in Quincy?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh yes, we always had a cow and raised a calf to butcher and one or two. We always raised fogs and chickens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it so different? What made it really different there as compared to pioneering?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: One of the differences perhaps, well maybe it wasn't so different but I hadn't thought of it that way, that these people had had good times financially. And they had just... And my folks had come out there with nothing. They'd take up a homestead to get free land. It was a different start entirely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they expect the good times to come back again pretty soon? Oh, at Wenatchee you mean?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, they hoped but then when a depression hits you don't know how long it's going to last. Fortunately it didn't last too long. But so most of them saved their arches now. It's a flourishing business again. And some of those same people are living there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With this aesthetics that you talked about, was that something you tried to consider in all the different projects if you could? Something to be artistic, to make things?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what homemaking is all about, isn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: It's got to be nourishing but it must be attractive and appealing. In the home, foods must be served tractively and tables must be set tractively. It isn't just enough to get food. You've got to feed the soul as well as the body, you know. Oh yes, it went through all phases of our work, of course.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But we actually applied our principles more to home -princing but it carried over.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That's how it could be a work of art just as much as a piece of needlework or a rug or anything else. The last thing you said, I was here before, that your mother had really tried to instill a sense of beauty in the kids on the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think you said that. Oh sure. So that you saw the beauty in the world when you were growing up and saw what things... Oh yes.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, probably more than lots of families did. I'm sure that was true.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: She enjoyed beauty in many forms. Poetry was a great thing in her life.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: She had a good sense of color and knew something about art but not the same way that I did with my training in home economics. I mean, you know, as far as I don't suppose she knew what the primary colors or that part, I don't think she'd ever had any training in that direction. But we taught our women that. We taught it from the basics, you know, mostly through clothing and home -princing but also in foods. It has to be that way. Well, I kind of had the idea that it was less of a practical matter, the way it meant when you were growing up and more one of value. What do you think was important about... I mean, much of you, out of you, just the practical side took so much. There wasn't much energy or time left for the other but it was always important.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask too, whether the furniture, this work and home furnishings, was that something that was important in the program? Did you really teach furniture making or furniture repair?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, we taught... The thing was that their homes had become a little bit run down, you see, and some of them very much so. And some of them had had real nice homes but they hadn't been able to keep them up. And we tried to teach them things like recovering chairs, upholstered furniture, we worked with that. Because they did have pieces that had been good, they'd have a Davenport that had been a good piece but it was all worn out. And we had work in reupholstering and recovering, slipcovers especially. They could afford to buy some material for slipcovers where they couldn't afford a new Davenport. And then we taught rug making, as I said, which brightened up their homes considerably and was practical as well as added. It was enjoyable work that they could do in their leisure time. They didn't make them part of this thing. And let's see, what other kind... And then the refinishing, we had them go into their attics and drag out their old chairs and we taught them to refinish furniture and cane. A lot of them had cane chairs, I taught them to cane chairs. I didn't know how but I taught myself those kind of things. Refinishing and in what way would they... Well, like the piece that I've done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like this?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, they'd have an old painted chair like this on the back porch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they'd take the paint off?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And we would study the chair and if it was worth it they would take the paint off and refinish the wood. And I mostly taught them the linseed oil and turpentine rubbing method which was acceptable. It was interesting to me that that was part of the thinking at that time, the idea of restoring the wood was still important then. And in the East, in extension work, they had excellent bulletins from a lot of the Midwest counties that had big extension programs more advanced than we had about here. And they had excellent bulletins all over this and we could get their bulletins to guide us in methods. And the girls did this too, the 4 -H girls, if they'd find an old chair then they would redo it for their room and that was part of their room improvement project. You see they'd make it valuable and now of course their antiques, they weren't so interested in them as antiques then as getting an interesting usable chair. And we would study the lines and if the chair was worth doing it, an attractive chair, a comfortable chair. And then they did some of that. But the value of the wood, the original wood was appreciated at the time. Oh yes. They thought it was better to have the wood than the paint which is something that's... Well, there was nothing wrong, sometimes they did paint furniture too, they painted furniture but the refinishing of good hardwoods was part of it. Study too. And they could do a real good job and their husbands sometimes helped with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what were you going to say about the sage and the homestead?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, at Quincy, now this is just from our family's standpoint again, we had four kinds of sagebrush in that valley, in that Quincy valley. And we had our own names for them and I thought maybe that was kind of interesting. And the one was the regular grey sagebrush that you would usually think of as sagebrush that's everywhere where there is sagebrush. And it has a kind of a yellow blossom in the spring and very fragrant bees make honey that some places they have sage honey they sell, you know. That was the predominant kind that was clear but amongst that grey sage there were three other kinds that we called sagebrush. And one we called prickly sagebrush. And it had actual thorns on the stems. And it grew about the same size as the grey sagebrush. And it had not a flower but I find now in studying flower books that it did have a little flower that was mostly stamens and then it had bracts that were different from leaves that came out. And they were somewhere between a quarter and three quarters of an inch sometimes. And they made a cluster which at a distance kind of looked like flowers and they were different colors. They were a lime shading from lime green as they matured I think they changed to orange and red so they were rather colorful. And as a child I tasted everything. Nowadays there are supposed to be a lot of poisonous plants but fortunately I guess I never hit one because I always tasted everything. And these bracts had kind of a sour like, what is this, miner's lettuce that we eat that has a bit of an oxalic taste to it. That's what they tasted like. Now in the flower book that bush is not a sagebrush it has a name. I think it's called hop sage.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: And I'm quite excited to find that it didn't have a name because we didn't know that we just named things you know. Yes hop sage it was called. And then the purple sage was the third one that we had and it was very much, the flower was very much like the garden sage. It had a big bunch of purple flowers. That's the way it looked. And it was very fragrant and the bees loved it and the plant itself was not as gray as the gray sage. We never used it for anything but it was great fun to run around through the sagebrush and find these different kinds when they bloom. Then the fourth kind we called feather bed sagebrush. And it was a yellow plant and now I find that it isn't sagebrush at all either but it's, no that's the purple thing. It's called rabbit brush in the desert country but we didn't know that. And the reason we called it feather bed sagebrush because it went to seed and made a fluffy soft plant as it went to seed. And when the men went out on the road to work and slept under the wagon with their bed roll there were no pine boughs or fur boughs like people in the woods do to make a bed. And I think that's what you know, like when you go camping and make your bed on boughs. There were no evergreens so the men would gather this, what we called feather bed sagebrush and put it under their beds and it was real soft and suggested a feather bed too. I think that's just kind of a cute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you call it purple sage?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We call that one purple sage, yes. Which is the other name for it. Which is the name for it. But because it was so purple the flowers were just like that and it looked quite a bit like garden sage but the leaves didn't taste like that. They tasted more like sagebrush and it is a sagebrush I guess. We didn't have greasewood, I noticed in that book there's greasewood but I used to think later I wondered if this prickly one was greasewood and it wasn't until I got this book that I knew for sure what it was. But those were the four that we used to have fun looking for in the fields around us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they together the main vegetation?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, then there were wildflowers amongst the sagebrush, lots of wildflowers and some grass. There was a little grass that came up in the spring called sheep grass because occasionally there would be herds of sheep go through and eat it in the early spring but it was a real fine little grass and it dried up early in that country and then in the summer there wasn't any grass. Then there was in the draws where there was some moisture there was this big rye grass and then there was bunch grass. There was some bunch grass yet too. There had been lots in the early days but we could still find bunch grass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that the bunch grass disappeared when it was grazed?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, I think it was grazed away and then the sagebrush takes over and the bunch grass recedes too. I don't know how far back there might have been lots of bunch grass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There wouldn't be together would there?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, there was bunch grass growing around through the sagebrush too. Some grass. Yes, there was still some but it was pretty much grazed off. I did want to ask you one other thing which is about here and that is what your impression was when you first came here at the house and if you had a, if the house really was as striking to you when you first saw it as it is to me when I see it come by and see it now. No, that isn't to be truthful. That wasn't the case. I was just newly married and Marvin's mother, this was her house, she loved this house and she was becoming elderly and Marvin had been looking after her and that was still necessary and the store was in bad circumstances. There wasn't a lot of money and we considered building a house and then we would still be looking after her but this great big house with just her living in it and so I don't know which, I suppose it was my suggestion that maybe we could make an apartment. We lived in an apartment upstairs. No, I didn't like this house. I thought it was ugly. I was not, I didn't like, like Lee does, I didn't like Victorian architecture at all and it wasn't the house that appealed to me. It was the family that I was marrying into and I liked his mother and she needed us and she, so I talked it over with her first before we were married and she thought that was a real good idea and she just turned over the rooms up there and let me have them fixed anyway. I wanted them and we fixed them real nice. They had a real nice little apartment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had kind of gone back to, in the meantime they aren't like we had them but it was real, real pretty, wasn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We had nice draw drapes at the windows and we had a nice rug on the floor and I collected a lot of this old furniture and partly because I liked to do, was interested in it but it was a way to get the furnishings that I could live with on the amount of money we had to spend. We could have bought new furniture but I wouldn't have been happy with the kind we would have had to buy and I was happy with this that I refinished and came and so forth and so that's the way it came about. And then now the house is so full of memories. The house is so full of memories now that of course I love it very dearly but I'm not in love with Victorian architecture. But you still are in love with it. No, no I still think that McConnell Manchin is ugly and I think this house is ugly, architecturally speaking. But younger people are just crazy about it and I don't know why. I had studied, we had to study architecture in our college course. I don't know, you asked me about what I took. That was one of our courses. We had to have a year in the architecture department. You shouldn't have done that. Well they didn't. Those teachers didn't by that time in the 20s, they weren't considering Victorian architecture beautiful either. It's ornate, it was ornate. We were being taught simpler. Yeah, this house was Victorian architecture, sure. I think so, yeah. It's an interesting house. It's the intimacy of it on the outside. It's interesting and the young people are just mad for it. Lee wants this house so bad you know he could die. What use he'd ever put it to I don't know because he gets your heart out of it. And the girl across the street, she's got a good job back in Philadelphia and she's just mad for this house. Don't ever let anybody have that house. It's a lot of young people. They like them. And it's been fun living here. We've had great fun. And then after his mother died we moved downstairs and remodeled again. We're still remodeling. But we've never done as much as we should. We never put in a proper heating system.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, when you left, when you came here married and you came here then, you didn't work then? Is that correct?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no. I helped at the school, I taught at the school I guess you'd call it teaching, it was a nightmare. But I helped at school one year during the war. They had hired a man. He was called in the army. After, just the school started and they were desperate and so I was certified. I taught in Idaho, I had a certificate. And I helped them out that year but it wasn't a very good experience. I had some work with 7th and 8th grade which I knew nothing about. That was worse than teaching Latin. And in the science class in high school that wasn't so bad because I'd had quite a lot of science of course. But 7th and 8th I knew nothing about 7th and 8th grade. It was terrible. And that's the most difficult age as far as boys are concerned. I've always been crazy about boys but it's a difficult age to, you don't get very well acquainted with, I was just teaching them half a day. We never had any real trouble, we got along but I wasn't a good teacher. I wasn't happy at doing it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you miss not having a career? Did you have any more?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no. I was so busy. She had a career. I should say I did. Well not in pain. I was trained in homemaking. She was. I think most. She was clearly a specialist. Most home economics women. Oh yeah. I met after she married and she didn't have that career. You were her career. Most home economics women I think looked forward to homemaking and some of them did both. Our home demonstration agent here now, you know, at Moscow has just had a baby, took time off and had a baby. But because that wasn't done in my day. I mean you didn't even get married and stay on the job. There were a few married widows though mostly. I don't think there were any married women that had jobs. Time off. Mrs. Anderson at Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me just really briefly about this clothing specialist position that you had afterwards?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Because that sounds like a lot. Well. Rather high level. There were, in the state office, there were specialists in each field. There was a nutritionist in one clothing. At my time I did a home furnishing too and then there was one in home management. She had a separate job. But mine included both home furnishing and clothing. And I worked with the 4 -H club leaders out in the counties and mostly with the home demonstration agents. I didn't work with the... I would go in and work with them and put on demonstrations for women's groups but mostly through local leaders. We would train the leaders, help them train the leaders in the counties. The work had progressed in most of the counties to that point where they were working through local leaders. That was true in both the home demonstration clubs and in the... now they don't call it that anymore. They call it all extension clubs. But 4 -H leaders, we would train 4 -H leaders. And we worked in lots of counties that had just men county agents that didn't have any women. And then we could help them more. And we worked with both women's groups and 4 -H groups. But we had... we set up a program offered and then they would choose what they wanted from it in either field. And we traveled out of Pullman all the time. We traveled at least two weeks out of every month and usually more. And went from county to county. Was that... A few days in each county. Was that traveling part... was that the... that sounds like kind of the center of the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean was that contact work that you were doing?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That was sort of the main... Yes, that's what it was. You didn't work in Pullman. You worked in the counties.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that traveling difficult?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, very difficult. It was more lonesome than to work in a county even. You see you were... you spent your nights on the train going across the state in the sleeper. Or you spent it getting in late to a hotel in a strange town where you didn't know anyone. And some of them worked long enough at it that they did work out recreational and social contacts but it was pretty hard to do. And of course when you were working with the home demonstration agents then sometimes there would be things you could do with them and that was better. But you were very seldom... you were almost always alone. Once in a while you and the nutritionists would be working together on some program and you might be traveling at the same time. But it didn't very often work out that way. That was the hardest part. Some of the girls liked it. I never did. It was interesting. You learned a lot about the country. You had lots of strange experiences. Small hotels. They were always cold. You always slept cold. Never slept warm in my life the whole time I did it. I always had cold feet. Didn't have enough sense to carry a hot water bottle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find people in the small towns who didn't tell you to be reserved?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well you never got acquainted with the people in the town. You went there and the county agent took you out to a meeting the next morning at 8 o 'clock and brought you back at 4 and you caught another bus down to the next place you were going to work. You didn't get acquainted in the towns at all. That would be difficult. Unless you already knew someone. If you had an old friend that lived there it would fight you out to dinner or something. Of course the county agent's wife sometimes would invite you out. But mostly you had the same old string beans. Small hotels and cafes. Every day. I don't know why they always had string beans but they did. We had lots of meals at our meetings. We had potluck meals at our meetings. We had lots of jello. Jello was one of the big items of our diet because potlucks are always jello and macaroni. That was our lunch quite often. Those kind of meals. Potluck meals that women served. They were good. They were good enough. In the evening we were usually on our own unless we had a meeting. But the work itself was really interesting.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Remember right after, how many years working for them?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We had to write bulletins and they were published there at the Prince Albert Plowman. We had to give radio talks sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it your responsibility as say, in the job at the clothing?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To make the decisions about what would be the new ideas presented to the state?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, yes. I guess so. We worked with the Home Economics Department in Pullman as much as we could. We were part of that. We were part of the Home Economics Department at the college. So that the clothing instructors there or the people in the textbook and clothing field would help us if we needed help. They sometimes had suggestions. This woman, this Catherine Bryden, did the teacher training. She worked with us a lot on a lot of practical things. She did all of this crafts work and all she was interested in. She taught a lot of those things that were helpful in 4 -H club work. Book bindings, soap making and candles and things like that that made the program more interesting and fun for the kids.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Then the counties themselves set up programs and we had to work with them in deciding what we were going to do. It was a joint decision, yes. We would make suggestions and then they would have needs that they wanted us to work on too. We would work it out together that way because it varied a great deal.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I've heard a lot about in the early days that women were often considered by men to be not as intellectual or not as... ...a women's sphere, that old idea that some people had before. What I was wondering is whether you ran into that as a professional working around the state. Whether there were times when men that were working above you or pilots who didn't respect you being in the field. I was in a strictly women's field. Maybe that made a difference, I don't know. I never ran into any of that. All the men that we worked with were great. Our college editor was great. We had to write lots of news stories. In Wenatchee we had to work with the reporters on the local papers. We had to have publicity. They were great. One of the reporters for the Wenatchee Daily World ran a column of mine. I was no Ann Landers or anything like that. But along that line, I used my picture and there was my little column and I gave recipes and household hints and all this stuff in the paper. That was awful good publicity. We had to have that. We had to work with lots of men that way. We had to work with the Chamber of Commerce and the county commissioners. We met all through this time when we were helping these people. We met for lunch with the county commissioners every week to get acquainted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the laboring people?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, during those depression years when we were trying to help the county with their problems of helping feed these people. We met for lunch with the county commissioners and that way we got acquainted. It was a business lunch and we talked business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was fairly often that you would meet?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Every week we'd meet with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: For a while we did, I'm sure, every week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you really felt that they accorded you all the respect for being able to make the decisions?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, I always felt like the queen of the crop. No, they treated me great.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your pay on par with the men who would have a similar position? Like the extension agent? With the county agent?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I don't know. I don't know for sure. He had had lots more experience so it shouldn't have been because I was a beginner, you see. No, I'm sure it wasn't as much. And they were all married men. That shouldn't have made any difference but it sort of did in those days. But because of his experience, he'd had years of experience at the time I worked with him. And I never worked with one county agent in the county. He was there when I came and there when I left. But I think his salary must have been more. I'm sure it must have been considerably more. It wouldn't have been fair if it hadn't been because of his years of experience. But none of us were really overpaid considering the hours we put in. I don't imagine. But for the times it was good. And when the banks closed and all, we managed some, you know, the banks closed during that time for a few days. We all managed somehow. And then our warrants were difficult to cash but we always had help. So we never suffered any.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Our warrants were easier to cash than the teachers. Some of the teachers had trouble with theirs but ours were not county warrants, I don't believe. It seems to me they came out of Pullman. That probably made a difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Marvin?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I wanted to ask you about two things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember that guy that used to be on the county that you used to bring out groceries to?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, Jersey Mail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, what's the story on him? How long did that go on that you'd done that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Come over here and tell him that story. Thank you. Sit over here and tell him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why don't you come over here so I can hear you?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Start at the beginning. Keep telling him the line. Well, wait.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear about him a little?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he happen to come here?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Tell that to start with. Well, he was a boxcar bomber. He had a gun. He wanted to, I guess, be doing something and he didn't want to ask for anything. He'd work.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He had come from down at Kennewick. He lived in the jungles in Kennewick. Yeah, they burned out the jungle down there. That's how he happened to get up here. He had worked up in this country as well. He was living there and he knew this country in his younger days. And he come up here and he settled up there a creek here and a wide place up there in the creek where it had quite a bit of brush and stuff.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What was that? You know that shack was there, wasn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, it was a cave at first. He lived in a cave. It was kind of dug out into the hill that protected himself. Yeah, that he dug out himself. Yeah, and made kind of a cave. The dump was up there. That's why he chose that spot. And there was a spring. So, this guy was here, got out of the dump and then you see the...

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What happened to the high water, didn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No. First you got him a cabin. You got him a chicken house. They brought down a chicken house and cleaned it up real good. Oh, yeah. One of the farm men was interested and moved him in this little cabin in the same spot. Yeah. He was a squatter. It was private land. They didn't object to his being there right next to the creek. That burned down then. That burned down one night. So the fellows went up there and fixed up a canvas and took a cot. He didn't want to move out, so they got him under shelter because of the rainy night, too.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He lived in Linden for a while, didn't he?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Not very long. You got him another cabin.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Don't you remember?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: The Fosters gave a cabin, a shed. That was a better one. They took up a tent for him right then. Yes, that night. And then Bob Marlin and Bob Magnuson and some of the fellows got him fixed up that night. We sent up some lights, some old quills. I had some old quills. See ya. People gave some bedding. And then you and Fred got this little building down here and fixed it.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Don't you remember?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Boy, you can get up there. It sounds like you really... And then he flooded out. See, a flood count. And he picked him up. He was sitting on the table. On the table. Yeah, it was flowing so slowly. He didn't dare drive right out. He got him out of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was right in the house, right?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Right around his barrack. It was clear. It went on the table.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Did the cabin stop? Did they stop?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, they took him up to the... Yeah. And that's when we took him up to the personal. That's where he's now.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Isn't he?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Just to show you how these officials are and how your money goes. Some things, they can be as penny -wise, you know. Pinchy. And other times, why they'll just throw it away, see. And I went up there and asked them first.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Was this before?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, they were giving you a little order, a little grocery order. A grocery, that's for his groceries. But I wanted increased. And, boy, I didn't get a little increase to him. Three dollars a week, I think. Got it up to about three dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what you were getting?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: It would be four dollars a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Four dollars a week?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, for a food farm. And what other stuff you'd happen to need. I'd always get something else, you know. And so when you flooded out, like, of course they had to take him to Moscow. So he's up there now in one of their homes. He's been there ever since. And the least it's cost him, I think, is about four hundred a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you give him what you wanted? Did you decide the food he'd give?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I decided everything. He wouldn't tell you nothing. He was out of it. Well, when the cabin burned the first time, they discovered a lot of unopened canned goods that he was hoarding. So after that, they were kind of, they didn't take him too much canned goods. I wouldn't take him no canned goods, no.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He'd just save it for a rainy day?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I took him stuff he had to cook or fix to eat for the week. People were awfully good at helping furnish his cabin. Yeah, they were. He had a little stove. Had a fellow that really kept me on the ball. It was Fred Sifl done. He's gone now.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They put a window in the cabin for him?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, they done let him that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, the crime garden, you know? His partner?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Mm -hmm. They done it. Or the carpenter. They kept it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the carpenter work?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They kept it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years was I to care?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He was there about fourteen years. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he'd come here after the Second World War then?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After that, was it over in front of him?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no, I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no, not after the Second. No, not after the war. No, he was sure during the war. During the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
