words: When they cleaned out the jungles down there at the cabin, remember that?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever work when he was here? Or did he go out?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He was too old to work. Too old. His younger days he had worked here. He worked, yeah. The one that was here that had worked. Yeah, yeah. He worked in the ship. He was kind of a monkey up in the log. He never had his hand out for nothing, you know. He never asked for nothing. He wasn't a bum.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What do you call it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He's regular bums, see.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He ate out of the dump?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Sure he was up there. People would take food up, good food, and leave it where he could find it. Lots of times. And then they'd take him special dinners on Thanksgiving and Christmas and all that. He went there on account of the spring and the dump. It was possible. But I took him. He was a good man. I had a little grocery order for him. County paid him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that after he'd been here for a while?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, yeah. He'd been here several years after he got that grocery order.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was that every week?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I'd take it up every Saturday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you give it to him?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: You bet. He'd take it to him for about, I think, four dollars a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he know where it was coming from or ask where it was coming from?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Never asked. He never asked my opinion.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Give it to him?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He'd go up to the radio department and play the car radio. He'd come out there and go up. Rock just came and transitioned to radio. He couldn't keep it running. He probably didn't know how to turn it off. The batteries ran down. Apparently he just loves it. He's up at Paradise Film. He just loves it. He was always interested in the baseball news. Now he can watch television. As long as I think... He has magazines. Oh, he thinks it's great. I started it out three dollars a month. No, three dollars a week. And ended up four dollars a week. I just went and took him out. And people would give him clothes. And he'd take it to him. And they're paying...

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I think it cost them, what is it, 175 dollars a month? Who knows?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: It's a lot of money. Three or four hundred a month. I'm sure. I'm not sure at the very least they could get. Well, they own this place, but they lease it out, see, for these old folks. They own this. County owns the old folks. No, he's in the other one, though. He's in the Paradise Film. The county has to pay for him. Just the same. Did you ever...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you carry on a conversation with him?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No. Only cause of the old folks. He'd answer your questions. He'd answer you. Yes or no. You weren't too personal. It wasn't too personal. They tried to take the census. They wanted to go on the census. The woman was taking the census. So she asked Marvin to go and get him on the census, and they did. Marvin got some facts about him, and I'm not sure, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really? He would answer questions for the census?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He wouldn't mean it. He didn't know what it was for. I'd say, well, I'm not sure. I don't know how. I'd use different ways, you know. You must have come here about so -and -so or something, whatever. I wouldn't ask him direct questions. He didn't expect him to answer, but I'd get him to answer. You know, he had to use it. Quite a guy. Oh, was he... Do you think he was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he slow? Was he what? Slow? Was he dumb?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No. Gosh, no. He was a fine old man. Fine old man. He just didn't... He was a circle stench. We don't know anything about his history. He was what they used to call a boxcar bum. Well, that period went out, and there was no more places for him. He just got caught in circumstances, I think. I would say that he might have had a good past and caught in bad circumstances. Yes, bad circumstances. That's something I didn't know. I never applied into it. But he was no more than he knew what was going on. But... It sounds like the townspeople kind of adopted him and didn't... I mean, he... No, he adopted us again. No, he never asked for anything. People couldn't let him starve up there. He never bummed or anything from house to house. You couldn't let anybody starve in your community. He grew up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you think people maybe enjoyed going and fixing up his house and that kind of thing?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, Marvin and these two men were all that did that. Yeah, others just friends of mine. After his fire, one woman brought down a big pile of blankets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, they were both dead, ain't they?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: But they were retired farmers. Yeah, one was a retired carton. One was a retired carton, the other was a retired farmer. And they were the ones that... But you were the guy that looked after him pretty much on a weekday. Yeah, but they looked after his wood, his fuel, and they was my helpers. And fixed his cabin up. See him after. They made him take a bath once too, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I think so. I didn't want to give personal facts, but you see. Now I never... All that, I saved the county hundreds of dollars there. Thousands. Yeah, and I never even got as much as to thank your... Well, you didn't have to do it. No, I didn't have to do it. But look at how much money I saved the county. He could have been there all them years. Well, they tried to take him once before. Yes. He wouldn't go. He wouldn't go. No, no, it wasn't the county fault, but they still saved the county money. He was entitled to county help. And they pay the way they run that county. I know how they run it. Exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see? They first, the Jerome County does, but they put the feeding out on the contract basis, see?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, he's not in that one, though. He's not in the Latah County one. He's in the Paradise Village, so they would have to pay. I would think. I don't know how it works, but I don't know how it works. The county has to pay it over there just the same. They have to pay a monthly. I don't know how they do what.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Do you suppose that's how it works?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Well, sure. I think so. They pay it in my room. They have to do it in the county one, too. They have to pay it for the feeding in for his room. Either way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was this just a few years ago?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: This was pretty recent that he got flooded out. I don't know. I don't know. A couple, three, five years. I don't know. Three or four years in a way. Yeah. Yeah. Well, then that little cabin burned down up here.

words: Was that Saturdays he used to take the food out to?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, every Saturday. Every Saturday. Didn't have to be Saturday, but it was. He had a little dog. He had a dog twice, three times. He lost the dog once. He was so handsome. Somebody picked him up and he was a dog. So then Herman had to find some bones for the dog.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Nobody begrudged him having a dog.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Hey, James. He was a human being, just saying. You or I. Well, he had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The other thing I was going to ask you about was, you were saying that you remembered when Frank Brockey used to come down here with his father? Frank Brockey? Yeah, when he used to come down to the store when he was a kid?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. I remember, well, you're the nicest picture I've got. This Frank here, talk about him, Troy. His father. His father was Frank also. I got a nice mental picture of him. Well, it's a mental scene. But Justice, he had his four cars here way back when they had a car. He'd come down with a team and he'd bring sort of how far back. Frank was just a little kid and Georgia was the oldest. And then Frank wasn't in that there with him. Then Wally and Ken. His four, that's all of them. Margaret was the girl. Margaret was the girl. But they always, they'd go into the shop and see him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the father was Frank?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The father was me and Frank? But anyway, these kids would see, keep saying, Daddy, Daddy or Daddy?

words: Don't forget Margaret. Right. They had to take Margaret to treat home every time they come. Them boys had to get her. Pretty good time they got through. They had pretty good sized treats she got, you know. But every Saturday, that was the nicest picture. Oh, he was a swell guy. This Frank Brockey. His father. Well, that'd be Frank Brockey's.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Troy, what would that be?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: His father. His father. Yeah, it'd be his father. Frank Brockey and Troy tells me about coming into the store. Into my store. Yeah, your store. And he says that he used to be scared, you know, going into the big town of Kendrick when he was a little boy. Is that, yeah. And they'd be right there. There was Wally and Frank and Ken. Three boys, see. George. George. George was... George was... A little too old. A little too old. And I said, well, whether he was with them or not. Yeah, I guess George was with them. Kenneth wasn't always with them because he was too young, maybe. No, Kenneth was with them. But anyway, they'd stand around there and I'd fill the brush with her. Nobody waited until their dad, Frank Brockey, on me. I was the only one in the store that could wait on him. They'd stand around and wait until I was through and then go and wait on him. He just understood that. No questions asked with the other clerks because nobody could wait on him. So they just got to have him and let him wait. Because that's what he'd do, see. Well, he just wanted you to wait on him. Yeah, Frank, the old Frank. Dad, we was pretty good friends, you know, pretty good pals, but he just... If he was going to trade there, he was going to have him wait on me. That's when I was trading up the other store. It wasn't a long store then. It was another store here in town.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: If you were working in, you mean?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, yeah, that's where I was working up the dam on Florence.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: That was the blues dam on Florence, huh?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Got a drag at the store. We were still on a trade deal with Tom. My brother, Tom, he's quite a trader. He had a chance to trade his Montana ranch off for this drag at the store down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's how you got it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, that's how I got it. I got it. Of course, Tom, it is Tom, but Tom and I, we always done a little trading amongst ourselves. We had a ranch together up here on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you trade him for the store?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, again, well eventually I got the store and he got the ranch. I don't know how you want to call it, but it's trading around. That's how they ended up anyway. No, there's no... It's fine, it's just the way... You bet it was all... Yeah, but he stopped from around here on the ranch. Yeah. Better for both of them. Better for both of them. Because now he's passed away saying it's a way to ruin it. All she's got to do is collect the thread on it. She's still got it. She's smart keeping it too. They did that with the cattle too. Yeah, with the cattle. I let him have the cattle and I took the store. Well, I have to get going. I see it's five and I got to get more. I hope you've got what you wanted. Oh, I think so. I've enjoyed...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What you want?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: I couldn't think what to tell you because it didn't seem like it's anything hard. It was... I put them down.

words: What'd you get?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: As I tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Martin Thomas?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Martin. He lived here now. He lived right across the street here on the corner. But anyway, he had this big ranch up here on its own band, the Coxlough. And these locust trees were raised up there in Grothe. And donated them to town. Every locust tree on the ranch from the other side was raised by Martin Thomas. They watered them with those trees. More strong watering tank crews to get them started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's quite a nice little story there, don't you see? Had they? Had they, Linda? Had they had locust trees before the fire or was it just after the fire?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the first time they were planted?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yes, they were planted. He raised a lot of their locusts, grove. He had a grove up there to raise these trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did he start right after the fire? Is that when they first came in here? Would it have been shortly after the fire?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They probably were on the ranch. They were grown after. We don't know. We haven't come to see exactly yet on when they were planted. They were grown after the fire. It was early. They planted them. They were up on a ranch. They were fired in the pit. Yeah. I don't know whether it was that bad. Andy Cox. I don't know whether he'd remember or not. And then there are... That's where they were raised. There's a crab -duffled tree up on the ridge. The place that was hosted by William Freytag in 1850, I think, way back in the lake. And that crab -duffled tree is still there and still productive. And our garden club members are going to find those kind of trees. And I'm going to include that single tree that is historic. And some of the native trees. In the picture of Old Ealing, there was a tree that is still a landmark. The old pictures and the town is gone, but that tree is still there. And it's sort of a guide as to where the buildings were. It's a big pine tree. And then Marlon and I have been talking. I've been getting to tell me about the coon orchards. The coon orchards and coon dryers planted two or three of them here. It's kind of nice that it's kind of there, I think. About their importance to the community. To the county, to the community. And now they're armed with coon orchards. Oh, God. There were lots of fruit trees planted here, but I didn't know how much we could find that would be really interesting. There are a few other kind of historic trees that are unusual that I mentioned. The garden club and what have you that's going on. We went look on Sunday to look at the pictures. I don't know what our, what they didn't do yet, exactly. Around that old castle down there. He's going to get rich on it. There's some trees that must be very rich. The coon orchards, the coon that planted it out, this orchard, they sold a crop off on you. He made a whole thing. Big pile of money. Dad was going to church. And the coon stopped until he bought the orchard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there was no market for them?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Had one good crop. And the factory picked them. They called it a coal storage plant over here. No one did music anymore. And he filled that full of apples, black apples. And next spring he would come down there. I just remember a kid then, I remember. And dump them to get the boxes back. Dump the apples. After growing them, ours was picking up agnes, almonds, you know. And that ended our footprint.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there no markets for them back east?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: No, there's no market anywhere. At least your family. My brother happened to hit it right. He had 48 more acres. He had 110 acres altogether. And he happened to hit a good crop and a big price. And he sold out. And Dad lost money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was your father's brother?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Father's brother -in -law. Yeah, his wife. My aunt's. My aunt's. Yeah, she was my aunt. His wife and my aunt.

words: The horses did establish myself. He never left. He had to see the daily papers. They'd have a list on there, you know. Missing, you know, action. He'd just call him every morning, this daily paper. And he couldn't leave until he'd read that missing column. You know what he was thinking about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I didn't have to ask him, did I?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: He had a son over there, my dad. But I say he couldn't leave town and do anything that day, go to work, until he got that daily paper and read over the list. Tom was in France.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: What?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Because Tom was in France. Yeah, he was using the lines.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was your father?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, and his father. My brother was in France. But my dad, that's how I expected him. We just didn't talk about it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's true that a lot of the people at Cameron didn't support the war, were against the war?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, of course they were. They were direct from Germany. They were from Germany. You would have been too. I would have. Their relatives were still there. They were relatives. They were in the front lines.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They looked at it probably a whole different way with the light. Someone did go and it turned out.

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: They couldn't blame the United States for it though, could they?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: We, we, we, I guess we, that war probably saved that bunch of them little countries over there, didn't it?

speaker: Martha Lowery Long or F. Marvin Long
words: If I remember right. Well, I've heard some of those folks say that they got a hard time from the American boys. You know, that they were looked at as being unpatriotic by... Oh, sure. But when I say this, it was too, too silent. But you see what...
